questions.question: What replaced the Estate Agency Affairs Board (EAAB) under the Property Practitioners Act 22 of 2019?

options.A: The Estate Agents Council of South Africa
options.B: The National Department of Human Settlements
options.C: The Property Practitioners Regulatory Authority (PPRA)
options.D: The South African Property Owners Association

questions.explanation: The PPRA replaced the EAAB when the Property Practitioners Act 22 of 2019 came into effect.

questions.question: Under the Act, what is the new name for the Estate Agents Fidelity Fund?

options.A: The National Property Fund
options.B: The SA Property Practitioners Trust
options.C: The Estate Agents Reserve Fund
options.D: The Property Practitioners Fidelity Fund

questions.explanation: The Act renamed the fund to align with new terminology.

questions.question: Which body was established to resolve disputes between property practitioners and consumers?

options.A: The Property Practitioners Tribunal
options.B: The National Consumer Commission
options.C: The Property Practitioners Ombud
options.D: The Master of the High Court

questions.explanation: The Act introduced the Property Practitioners Ombud as a new dispute resolution mechanism.

questions.question: Which professional is EXCLUDED from the definition of 'property practitioner'?

options.A: A rental agent working for a letting company
options.B: A business broker selling going concern businesses
options.C: An attorney selling property in the course of their practice
options.D: A managing agent managing a sectional title scheme

questions.explanation: Attorneys are regulated by the Legal Practice Council and are specifically excluded.

questions.question: A practitioner without a valid FFC conducts transactions. What is the consequence?

options.A: They receive a written warning
options.B: Their commission is reduced by 50%
options.C: They are not entitled to claim commission
options.D: Their agency licence is suspended for 30 days

questions.explanation: Section 50 of the Act — no valid FFC means no entitlement to commission.

questions.question: What happens if the mandatory disclosure form is not provided by the seller?

options.A: The agent must complete the form on behalf of the seller
options.B: The transaction may proceed if both parties agree
options.C: The property practitioner may not accept the mandate
options.D: The agent must report this to the PPRA within 7 days

questions.explanation: Section 67 of the Act prohibits accepting a mandate without the mandatory disclosure form.

questions.question: For how many years must a property practitioner retain all business records?

options.A: 3 years
options.B: 7 years
options.C: 10 years
options.D: 5 years

questions.explanation: Section 55 of the Act prescribes a 5-year document retention period.

questions.question: Which of the following is a prescribed disqualification for holding an FFC?

options.A: Having been retrenched in the past 2 years
options.B: Owning a property in your personal capacity
options.C: Not having a valid tax clearance certificate
options.D: Being over 65 years of age

questions.explanation: The Act introduced not having a valid tax clearance certificate as a disqualification ground.

questions.question: The PPRA is governed by which structure?

options.A: The Minister of Human Settlements directly
options.B: An elected committee of senior practitioners
options.C: A Board known as the Board of the Authority
options.D: The National Treasury

questions.explanation: The PPRA is governed and acts through its Board as prescribed in the Act.

questions.question: What must a property practitioner include in all marketing material?

options.A: Their NQF level of qualification
options.B: The name of their principal agent
options.C: Their bank account details for deposits
options.D: The words 'Registered with the PPRA' and their FFC number

questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct requires practitioners to indicate their registration status on all communications.

questions.question: What document was introduced by the Act to protect buyers about property condition?

options.A: The property transfer certificate
options.B: The offer to purchase addendum
options.C: The mandatory disclosure form
options.D: The deeds registry compliance certificate

questions.explanation: The mandatory disclosure form requires sellers to disclose all known defects.

questions.question: A practitioner conducts business without a valid FFC for 2 months. Can they claim commission?

options.A: Yes, provided the deals were properly documented
options.B: Yes, if the PPRA approves a backdated FFC
options.C: No — they are not entitled to any commission
options.D: Only half the commission may be claimed

questions.explanation: The Act is absolute — no valid FFC means no entitlement to commission regardless of circumstances.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the PPRA and the National Department of Human Settlements?

options.A: The PPRA falls directly under the National Treasury
options.B: The PPRA operates independently with no government oversight
options.C: The PPRA falls under the mandate of the National Department of Human Settlements
options.D: The PPRA reports directly to the President

questions.explanation: The PPRA is a regulatory body operating under the National Department of Human Settlements.

questions.question: Which of the following activities falls within the definition of a 'property practitioner'?

options.A: A person who sells their own personal property
options.B: An attorney who sells a property as part of estate administration
options.C: A managing agent who manages a sectional title scheme
options.D: A bank employee processing bond applications

questions.explanation: Managing agents are now included in the definition and must register with the PPRA.

questions.question: Unclaimed trust money must be paid over to the Fidelity Fund after how many years?

options.A: 1 year
options.B: 2 years
options.C: 3 years
options.D: 5 years

questions.explanation: The Property Practitioners Act prescribes 3 years for unclaimed trust money.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of the PPRA?

options.A: Issuing Fidelity Fund Certificates
options.B: Regulating the education and training of property practitioners
options.C: Registering property transfer deeds at the Deeds Office
options.D: Investigating complaints against property practitioners

questions.explanation: Registering property deeds is the function of the Deeds Registry, not the PPRA.

questions.question: What is the minimum pass rate required for the NQF Level 4 Real Estate qualification?
questions.explanation: The NQF4 Real Estate qualification requires a minimum pass rate of 65%.

questions.question: What must happen before a conveyancer can pay commission to a property practitioner?

options.A: The practitioner must submit a signed invoice
options.B: The practitioner must confirm their bank account details
options.C: The practitioner must hold a valid FFC at the time the sale agreement was concluded
options.D: The practitioner must provide a copy of their NQF4 certificate

questions.explanation: The Act is explicit — the conveyancer may not pay commission unless the practitioner held a valid FFC.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Property Practitioners Fidelity Fund?

options.A: To fund the PPRA's operating costs
options.B: To pay property practitioners their salaries
options.C: To provide bursaries for NQF4 students
options.D: To protect the public against financial losses caused by dishonest property practitioners

questions.explanation: The Fidelity Fund compensates the public for losses from fraudulent or dishonest practitioners.

questions.question: When must a property practitioner apply for their annual FFC renewal?

options.A: Before 31 December each year
options.B: Before 28 February each year
options.C: Before 31 October each year
options.D: Before 30 June each year

questions.explanation: Property practitioners must apply for FFC renewal before 31 October each year.

questions.question: A candidate property practitioner registers for the first time. What fee do they pay towards the Fidelity Fund?
questions.explanation: Upon first registration, every property practitioner pays R400 towards the Fidelity Fund.

questions.question: How often must a principal property practitioner's trust account be audited?

options.A: Every 3 years
options.B: Every 2 years
options.C: Only when requested by the PPRA
options.D: Annually

questions.explanation: Trust accounts must be audited annually with the audit report submitted within 6 months of year-end.

questions.question: A practitioner's FFC has expired. A client signs an offer to purchase through them the next day. What is the legal position?

options.A: The commission is claimable but reduced by 20%
options.B: The commission is claimable if the FFC is renewed within 30 days
options.C: The practitioner has no legal right to claim commission
options.D: The commission is held in trust until the FFC is renewed

questions.explanation: An expired FFC means the practitioner is unregistered and not entitled to commission.

questions.question: A practitioner is found guilty of fraud. What happens to their FFC?

options.A: It is suspended for 6 months
options.B: It is endorsed with a fraud notation
options.C: Nothing — the criminal courts deal with this separately
options.D: It may be withdrawn by the PPRA

questions.explanation: The PPRA has the power to withdraw an FFC on disciplinary or criminal grounds.

questions.question: Which person is required to hold an FFC under the Act?

options.A: A bank employee who processes bond applications
options.B: A managing agent who collects rental on behalf of landlords
options.C: An attorney's conveyancing secretary
options.D: A valuer working for a municipality

questions.explanation: Managing agents are now classified as property practitioners and must hold a valid FFC.

questions.question: A non-principal with an FFC issued before 1 July 2024 must complete which exam to maintain full status?

options.A: The NQF5 exam
options.B: The PDE4 (Professional Designation Examination, Level 4)
options.C: The SSETA competency assessment
options.D: The PPRA Ethics exam

questions.explanation: Non-principals holding an NQF4 under the legacy system must write and pass the PDE4.

questions.question: A practitioner opens a new trust account. What must they do immediately?

options.A: Notify their clients in writing within 7 days
options.B: Advertise the new account in a local newspaper
options.C: Notify the PPRA in writing of the account opening and the appointed auditor
options.D: Submit the first month's bank statement to the PPRA

questions.explanation: The Act requires notification to the PPRA when a trust account is opened and an auditor is appointed.

questions.question: What is the current annual FFC fee for a registered non-principal (approximately)?

options.D: R780 per annum (R2 340 for 3 years)

questions.explanation: The FFC fee is approximately R780 per annum or R2 340 for a 3-year certificate.

questions.question: Can a single FFC be issued to a natural person acting in more than one capacity?

options.A: No — a separate FFC is required for each capacity
options.B: Only if both capacities are within the same agency
options.C: Yes — a single FFC is issued covering all capacities
options.D: Only with written permission from the PPRA

questions.explanation: The Act provides that a single FFC is issued to a natural person regardless of how many capacities they operate in.

questions.question: Which of the following would NOT disqualify a person from holding an FFC?

options.A: Having been declared insolvent without rehabilitation
options.B: Having been convicted of fraud in the past
options.C: Owning more than one property in their personal capacity
options.D: Not being in possession of a valid tax clearance certificate

questions.explanation: Owning property personally is not a disqualifying factor under the Act.

questions.question: What is the consequence for a conveyancer who pays commission to a practitioner without a valid FFC?

options.A: The conveyancer must report themselves to the PPRA
options.B: The payment is valid but must be declared to SARS
options.C: The conveyancer commits a contravention of the Act
options.D: The conveyancer must withhold 10% as a penalty

questions.explanation: The Act places an obligation on conveyancers not to pay commission unless the practitioner held a valid FFC.

questions.question: Trust account records must be balanced and administered how often?

options.A: Annually
options.B: Quarterly
options.C: Weekly
options.D: Monthly

questions.explanation: The Act requires separate trust accounting records to be balanced monthly.

questions.question: A practitioner wants to transfer sale proceeds to a seller before property is registered. Is this permissible?

options.A: Yes, if both parties agree in writing
options.B: Yes, if the conveyancer approves it
options.C: No — the Act prohibits including such a clause in an agreement
options.D: Yes, if a bank guarantee is provided

questions.explanation: The Property Practitioners Act specifically prohibits any clause allowing pre-registration payment of sale proceeds.

questions.question: A trust account audit report must be submitted to the PPRA within how many months of the financial year-end?

options.A: 3 months
options.B: 4 months
options.C: 6 months
options.D: 9 months

questions.explanation: Both business and trust account audit reports must be submitted within 6 months of year-end.

questions.question: Money held in a practitioner's trust account may be released when:

options.A: The property is listed on the market
options.B: The mandate agreement is signed by all parties
options.C: The seller requests payment in writing
options.D: The practitioner is lawfully instructed to make payment or the transaction is finalised

questions.explanation: Trust money must be retained until the practitioner is lawfully instructed to release it.

questions.question: What is a 'sole mandate' in South African property law?

options.A: A mandate issued exclusively to a foreign agent
options.B: A mandate granting one agency the exclusive right to sell or let a property for a defined period
options.C: A mandate that requires the seller to sell at a fixed price
options.D: A mandate that can only be signed in front of a notary

questions.explanation: A sole mandate gives one agency exclusive right to market and sell/let the property for an agreed period.

questions.question: What is the legal requirement for a valid mandate?

options.A: It must be signed in front of two witnesses
options.B: It must be notarially attested
options.C: It must be submitted to the PPRA for approval
options.D: It must be in writing and signed by the seller or lessor

questions.explanation: A mandate must be in writing and signed to be legally enforceable.

questions.question: A seller gives a written sole mandate to Agency A. While active, the seller independently sells the property. What is Agency A's position?

options.A: Agency A loses all rights to commission
options.B: Agency A can only claim 50% commission
options.C: Agency A is entitled to claim commission because the sole mandate was still active
options.D: Agency A must prove they introduced the buyer

questions.explanation: A sole mandate entitles the agency to commission even if the owner sells themselves during the mandate period.

questions.question: Under Section 67 of the Act, a practitioner MUST NOT accept a mandate unless:

options.A: The seller has obtained a bond cancellation figure
options.B: The seller has provided three comparable sales
options.C: The seller has produced a duly completed and signed mandatory disclosure form
options.D: The seller has obtained a rates clearance certificate

questions.explanation: Section 67 is clear — no mandatory disclosure form, no mandate.

questions.question: Which BEST describes an 'open mandate'?

options.A: A mandate with no fixed price
options.B: A mandate requiring all offers to be submitted publicly
options.C: A mandate given to multiple agencies simultaneously where commission is paid to the one who concludes the sale
options.D: A mandate that can be cancelled without notice

questions.explanation: An open mandate allows the seller to appoint multiple agents; the agent who produces a successful buyer earns commission.

questions.question: For a contract of sale of immovable property to be valid, which is NOT a requirement?

options.A: All material terms must be in writing
options.B: The contract must be signed by both parties
options.C: The seller must be the lawful owner
options.D: The contract must be witnessed by a commissioner of oaths

questions.explanation: A sale of immovable property does not need to be witnessed by a commissioner of oaths.

questions.question: What is 'effective cause' in the context of commission claims?

options.A: The emotional reason behind a buyer's decision
options.B: The direct or proximate cause that brought about the conclusion of the sale
options.C: The legal basis for a commission dispute
options.D: The method used to calculate commission

questions.explanation: Effective cause refers to the link between the agent's actions and the concluded sale.

questions.question: Agent A introduces Buyer X to a property. Buyer X later buys directly from the seller without Agent A. Which is TRUE?

options.A: Agent A has no claim as they were not present at the sale
options.B: Agent A can claim only if they have a sole mandate
options.C: Agent A may have a claim if they can prove they were the effective cause of the sale
options.D: Agent A must share the commission with the seller

questions.explanation: Effective cause is critical — if the agent's introduction led to the sale they may be entitled to commission.

questions.question: A practitioner receives a written mandate. The seller later verbally instructs them to stop marketing. What should the practitioner do?

options.A: Immediately stop all marketing activities
options.B: Continue marketing because verbal instructions are not binding on a written mandate
options.C: Request a written cancellation of the mandate before ceasing activities
options.D: Refer the matter to the PPRA

questions.explanation: A written mandate should ideally be cancelled in writing to protect the practitioner's legal position.

questions.question: A seller discloses a serious structural defect verbally but asks the agent not to tell buyers. What must the agent do?

options.A: Keep the information confidential as instructed
options.B: Disclose the defect only if the buyer specifically asks
options.C: Withdraw from the mandate immediately without explanation
options.D: Refuse to conceal the defect — full disclosure to potential buyers is required

questions.explanation: Concealing known material defects is a violation of the Code of Conduct.

questions.question: What is 'commission castration'?

options.A: An agent losing their FFC due to ethical violations
options.B: A seller refusing to pay commission after a successful sale
options.C: An agent being denied their rightful commission through deliberate manipulation
options.D: A buyer cancelling a sale to avoid paying transfer costs

questions.explanation: Commission castration refers to deliberate attempts to cut the agent out of rightfully earned commission.

questions.question: A practitioner signs a sole mandate with no end date. What is the legal position?

options.A: The mandate is automatically valid for 12 months
options.B: The mandate is valid until the property is sold
options.C: The mandate may be challenged — a clear start and end date is recommended for enforceability
options.D: The mandate defaults to an open mandate

questions.explanation: Without an end date the mandate's enforceability becomes questionable.

questions.question: Who has the authority to sign a mandate on behalf of a company selling its property?

options.A: Any director of the company
options.B: The company's accountant
options.C: A duly authorised representative of the company, supported by a resolution
options.D: The company's attorney only

questions.explanation: A company must be represented by someone duly authorised by a board resolution.

questions.question: A sale is concluded. The seller later cancels due to the buyer's breach. Is the practitioner still entitled to commission?

options.A: No — commission is only payable upon transfer
options.B: No — the cancellation cancels all obligations
options.C: Possibly yes — it depends on the terms of the mandate and sale agreement
options.D: Yes — commission is always payable upon conclusion of a valid sale agreement

questions.explanation: Whether commission is payable depends on the mandate terms and at what stage commission becomes due.

questions.question: The mandate specifies 5% commission on R1 000 000. Buyer and seller reduce the price to R950 000 without informing the agent. What commission is the agent entitled to?

options.B: No commission, as the agent was excluded from the negotiation
options.C: 5% of R1 000 000 — the original mandated price — unless the agent agreed to the reduction
options.D: 2.5% as a compromise

questions.explanation: The mandate fixes the commission — if the price was reduced without the agent's consent they may still be entitled to commission based on the original mandated price.

questions.question: What type of account must a property practitioner open to hold client funds?

options.A: A business current account
options.B: A savings account in the practitioner's personal name
options.C: A section 54(1)(a) trust account
options.D: A money market account in the agency's name

questions.explanation: The Act requires property practitioners to open a trust account as prescribed under section 54(1)(a).

questions.question: A practitioner places a deposit into their personal business account by mistake. What must they do?

options.A: Leave it there until transfer
options.B: Pay it back to the buyer and request a new payment
options.C: Immediately transfer the funds to the trust account
options.D: Notify the PPRA within 48 hours

questions.explanation: Client funds must be held in trust at all times — mixing trust and business funds is a serious breach.

questions.question: A practitioner earns interest on a trust savings account. To whom does this interest belong?

options.A: The property practitioner
options.B: The PPRA
options.C: The party entitled to it, as agreed in writing — if not agreed, it accrues to the Fidelity Fund
options.D: The bank holding the account

questions.explanation: The Act prohibits practitioners from retaining interest on trust funds without agreement.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the annual trust account audit?

options.A: To calculate the practitioner's income tax liability
options.B: To assess the agent's CPD compliance
options.C: To verify that all client funds are accounted for and managed in accordance with the Act
options.D: To determine the agency's annual commission income

questions.explanation: The annual audit verifies that client funds are properly safeguarded in compliance with the Act.

questions.question: A deposit of R200 000 is held in trust. The sale falls through due to the seller's breach. To whom must it be refunded?

options.A: To the seller as compensation
options.B: Split equally between buyer and seller
options.C: Retained by the agent as commission
options.D: To the buyer, as the seller was in breach

questions.explanation: If the seller breaches the agreement, the buyer is generally entitled to a full refund of their deposit.

questions.question: A practitioner holds R300 000 in trust from a deceased estate transaction 4 years ago. No valid claim has been received. What must happen?

options.A: They remain in the trust account indefinitely
options.B: They are paid to the Master of the High Court
options.C: They must be paid over to the Property Practitioners Fidelity Fund after 3 years
options.D: They are transferred to the agent's business account

questions.explanation: The Act requires unclaimed trust money to be paid to the Fidelity Fund after 3 years.

questions.question: Which is NOT permissible in respect of a trust account?

options.A: Holding buyer deposits pending transfer
options.B: Paying approved disbursements from trust
options.C: Earning interest on a trust savings account
options.D: Using trust funds to pay the agency's staff salaries

questions.explanation: Trust funds belong to clients and must not be used for any business expenses including salaries.

questions.question: Can a property practitioner operate without a trust account?

options.A: No — a trust account is always mandatory
options.B: Yes — if their turnover is below R1 million per year
options.C: Yes — if they obtain a trust account exemption from the PPRA
options.D: Yes — managing agents are exempt by default

questions.explanation: The PPRA can grant trust account exemptions in certain circumstances.

questions.question: A buyer's R50 000 deposit is placed in trust on Monday. When may these funds be released?

options.A: Immediately, with the seller's verbal authorisation
options.B: After 48 hours
options.C: After 7 business days
options.D: Only when lawfully instructed to do so, typically after registration of transfer

questions.explanation: Trust funds may only be released when the practitioner is lawfully instructed to do so.

questions.question: A principal's financial year ends on 28 February 2025. What is the latest trust account audit submission date?

options.B: 31 July 2025
options.C: 31 August 2025
options.D: 31 October 2025

questions.explanation: The audit report must be submitted within 6 months of year-end — February year-end means August deadline.

questions.question: A practitioner invests trust money in an interest-bearing account. What must they do with the interest earned?

options.A: Retain it as part of their commission income
options.B: Use it to cover trust administration costs
options.C: Donate it to the PPRA education fund
options.D: Pay the full interest amount to the party entitled to it, as agreed in writing

questions.explanation: The Act requires that all interest earned on invested trust money be paid in full to the party entitled to it.

questions.question: A practitioner fails to submit their trust account audit on time. What is the likely consequence?

options.A: A fine of R5 000 is automatically levied
options.B: Their commission for the year is forfeited
options.C: Their FFC renewal may be refused or their existing FFC withdrawn
options.D: They receive a written warning for the first offence

questions.explanation: Non-compliance with audit requirements can result in the refusal to issue or renew an FFC.

questions.question: Trust account records must be balanced how often?

options.A: Weekly
options.B: Quarterly
options.C: Annually
options.D: Monthly

questions.explanation: The Act requires trust account records to be kept separately and balanced monthly.

questions.question: A property practitioner holds rental deposits for 30 tenants. The agency is sold to a new owner. What must happen to the trust funds?

options.A: They automatically transfer to the new owner's account
options.B: They are paid out to all tenants immediately
options.C: They must be formally transferred to the new agency's trust account with proper authorisation
options.D: They are held by the bank until a court order is obtained

questions.explanation: Trust funds belong to clients and cannot simply be transferred without proper authorisation.

questions.question: Which best describes the principle behind a trust account?

options.A: It allows the agency to earn interest on client funds
options.B: It simplifies the agency's tax obligations
options.C: It ensures client funds are kept separate from the agency's own money and protected
options.D: It allows clients to access their funds at any time

questions.explanation: The trust account's fundamental purpose is to protect client funds by keeping them entirely separate.

questions.question: A property practitioner must act in whose best interest at all times?

options.A: The principal's interest
options.B: The buyer's interest
options.C: The client's interest, while acting honestly and lawfully
options.D: Their own financial interest

questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct requires practitioners to act honestly, fairly, and in the best interest of their client.

questions.question: A practitioner shares confidential client financial information with a third party without permission. This is a violation of:

options.A: The National Credit Act
options.B: The Competition Act
options.C: The Code of Conduct for Property Practitioners
options.D: The Consumer Protection Act only

questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct prohibits divulging confidential client information to third parties without just cause.

questions.question: A practitioner advertises a property well below market value without the seller's consent. This constitutes:

options.A: Acceptable competitive marketing
options.B: A breach of the National Consumer Commission guidelines only
options.C: A violation of the mandate and the Code of Conduct
options.D: Normal practice provided the seller is notified within 48 hours

questions.explanation: Advertising a property at an unauthorised price violates both the mandate agreement and ethical obligations.

questions.question: What is a 'conflict of interest' in a property transaction?

options.A: A disagreement between buyer and seller about price
options.B: An agent representing two agencies at the same time
options.C: A situation where the agent's personal interests may compromise their duty to their client
options.D: When an agent operates in more than one suburb

questions.explanation: A conflict of interest arises when the agent's personal gain could compromise their ability to act for the client.

questions.question: A practitioner fails to disclose that they have a personal interest in a property they are marketing. This is:

options.A: Acceptable if the price is fair market value
options.B: Only an issue if the buyer suffers financial loss
options.C: A serious ethical violation that must be disclosed upfront
options.D: Permitted if the practitioner is a candidate agent

questions.explanation: Practitioners must disclose any personal interest in a property transaction before proceeding.

questions.question: A practitioner deliberately provides a falsely high property valuation to secure the mandate. This is:

options.A: Optimistic pricing
options.B: Market positioning
options.C: Overpricing to win the mandate — an unethical practice
options.D: A standard negotiation tactic

questions.explanation: Deliberately inflating a valuation to secure a mandate is unethical and harms the seller.

questions.question: A practitioner is asked by their principal to commit a fraudulent act. What must the practitioner do?

options.A: Follow the instruction — the principal takes full legal responsibility
options.B: Carry out the instruction but document their objection in writing
options.C: Report the matter to the PPRA only after the transaction
options.D: Refuse to comply — each practitioner is personally accountable under the Code of Conduct

questions.explanation: A practitioner cannot hide behind instructions from a principal — they are personally bound by the Code of Conduct.

questions.question: What is the ethical duty of a practitioner when they know about a latent defect?

options.A: Disclose it only if specifically asked by the buyer
options.B: Inform the seller only, as they are the client
options.C: Include a general disclaimer in the sale agreement
options.D: Disclose all known material defects to all parties in the transaction

questions.explanation: The duty of disclosure extends to all parties — concealing known material defects is an ethical violation.

questions.question: Which best describes 'good faith' in a property transaction?

options.A: Both parties paying their legal fees promptly
options.B: The bank approving the buyer's bond application
options.C: Acting honestly, without concealment or deception, in all dealings
options.D: Completing the transaction within the agreed timeframe

questions.explanation: Good faith requires all parties and practitioners to act honestly and transparently.

questions.question: A practitioner accepts a gift of R5 000 from a buyer, without the seller's knowledge, after negotiating a lower price. This is:

options.A: Acceptable — agents are entitled to tips
options.B: Acceptable only if it is declared to SARS
options.C: A minor ethical lapse but not illegal
options.D: A serious breach of the Code of Conduct and potentially a criminal offence

questions.explanation: Accepting undisclosed payments from any party creates a clear conflict of interest.

questions.question: A practitioner's marketing states: 'We guarantee you will sell your home within 30 days.' Is this acceptable?

options.A: Yes, it is a standard marketing claim
options.B: Yes, if the practitioner is willing to back it up
options.C: Only if approved by the PPRA
options.D: No — making guarantees that cannot be substantiated violates ethical advertising standards

questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct requires that all advertising be truthful and not misleading.

questions.question: A practitioner acts as agent for both buyer and seller in the same transaction without disclosing this. This is:

options.A: Dual representation
options.B: Split agency
options.C: Undisclosed dual agency — an ethical violation
options.D: An open mandate arrangement

questions.explanation: Representing both parties without disclosure creates an undisclosed conflict of interest.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Code of Conduct for Property Practitioners?

options.A: To regulate the commission rates charged by agents
options.B: To prescribe the format of sale agreements
options.C: To set minimum standards of professional and ethical behaviour for property practitioners
options.D: To outline the tax obligations of estate agencies

questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct establishes the ethical and professional standards all practitioners must uphold.

questions.question: A practitioner learns their agency is being investigated. They shred all client files. This constitutes:

options.A: A reasonable precaution
options.B: A minor administrative error
options.C: Only a problem if the PPRA formally requests the documents
options.D: Obstruction of a regulatory investigation and a serious criminal and ethical violation

questions.explanation: Destroying documents during an investigation is obstruction of justice.

questions.question: A practitioner makes a factual error in a property listing that misleads a buyer who suffers financial loss. What is the practitioner's position?

options.A: Not liable as errors are covered by the mandate disclaimer
options.B: Only liable if the error was deliberate
options.C: The seller is solely liable as they provided the property information
options.D: The practitioner may be held liable for misrepresentation and could face disciplinary action

questions.explanation: Property practitioners have a duty of care to ensure accuracy in all property listings.

questions.question: The Deeds Registries Act 47 of 1937 was enacted primarily to:

options.A: Regulate the transfer duty payable on property sales
options.B: Establish the PPRA as a regulatory body
options.C: Ensure the security and indisputability of title to immovable property
options.D: Set minimum standards for property construction

questions.explanation: The Deeds Registries Act governs the registration of rights in immovable property.

questions.question: Land may only be registered in the Deeds Registry when:

options.A: The buyer has obtained bond approval
options.B: The seller has vacated the property
options.C: A diagram of the property has been approved
options.D: The estate agent's commission has been paid

questions.explanation: Among the requirements for Deeds Registry registration is an approved diagram of the property.

questions.question: Transfer duty must be paid to SARS:

options.A: Before the offer to purchase is signed
options.B: Within 30 days of the sale being concluded
options.C: After registration of transfer in the buyer's name
options.D: Before registration can take place — it is a condition for registration

questions.explanation: Transfer duty is a pre-condition for the registration of a property transfer.

questions.question: What is a 'servitude' in property law?

options.A: A clause in a lease agreement allowing the landlord entry at any time
options.B: A restriction preventing the property from being sold
options.C: A right that a person or entity has over another person's property
options.D: A tax levied on commercial properties

questions.explanation: A servitude is a real right that limits an owner's use of their property for the benefit of another.

questions.question: What is a 'praedial servitude'?

options.A: A servitude that benefits a specific individual personally
options.B: A servitude that benefits a neighbouring property, not a specific person
options.C: A servitude that expires after 10 years
options.D: A servitude registered exclusively in favour of a municipality

questions.explanation: A praedial servitude attaches to land — it benefits one piece of land and burdens another.

questions.question: When is a buyer required to pay the purchase price in terms of common law?

options.A: In full before the offer to purchase is signed
options.B: In full immediately after transfer
options.C: In full upon demand by the seller
options.D: In full on transfer

questions.explanation: Under common law, the purchase price for immovable property is payable in full on transfer.

questions.question: A property is sold 'voetstoots.' What does this mean for the buyer?

options.A: The seller guarantees the property is free of all defects
options.B: All legal costs are included in the purchase price
options.C: The property is sold as-is, and the seller is not liable for patent defects
options.D: The buyer waives their right to a property inspection

questions.explanation: Voetstoots means the property is sold in its existing condition — the seller is not liable for patent defects.

questions.question: What is the legal effect of a 'suspensive condition' in a sale agreement?

options.A: It immediately cancels the agreement
options.B: It gives the seller the right to withdraw within 5 days
options.C: The agreement only becomes binding if and when the condition is fulfilled
options.D: It suspends transfer duty until the condition is met

questions.explanation: A suspensive condition means the sale is not legally binding until the condition is met.

questions.question: A property is sold with a registered right of way servitude the buyer was not informed about. What is the buyer's position?

options.A: The seller must remove the servitude before transfer
options.B: The buyer can cancel the sale and claim damages
options.C: The buyer is bound by the servitude — registered servitudes follow the land
options.D: The estate agent is liable for the buyer's losses

questions.explanation: Registered servitudes are disclosed in the title deed — a buyer takes property subject to existing registered servitudes.

questions.question: What does 'transfer' of immovable property mean in South African law?

options.A: The physical handover of keys to the buyer
options.B: The signing of the offer to purchase by both parties
options.C: The payment of the purchase price into trust
options.D: The formal registration of ownership in the buyer's name at the Deeds Registry

questions.explanation: In South African law, ownership of immovable property only passes upon formal registration at the Deeds Registry.

questions.question: A property practitioner must give a buyer a copy of what document before concluding a sale?

options.A: The agency's trust account statement
options.B: The property's municipal rates account
options.C: The mandatory disclosure form completed by the seller
options.D: The PPRA's Code of Conduct

questions.explanation: The mandatory disclosure form must be made available to prospective buyers before a sale is concluded.

questions.question: What does CPD stand for in the context of the PPRA?

options.A: Commercial Property Development
options.B: Certificate in Property Dynamics
options.C: Continuing Professional Development
options.D: Certified Property Dealer

questions.explanation: CPD refers to the ongoing training requirements that registered property practitioners must fulfil annually.

questions.question: A practitioner's marketing material does not include their FFC number. What does this constitute?

options.A: A minor administrative oversight with no consequences
options.B: Acceptable if the FFC is valid and up to date
options.C: A breach only if a client complains
options.D: A violation of the Code of Conduct and PPRA regulations

questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct requires practitioners to include their FFC details on all marketing materials.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Alienation of Land Act in the context of property sales?

options.A: To regulate how foreign nationals may buy land
options.B: To prevent properties from being sold below market value
options.C: To regulate instalment sale agreements for the purchase of land
options.D: To set minimum standards for property valuations

questions.explanation: The Alienation of Land Act governs instalment sale agreements where the buyer pays in instalments before transfer.

questions.question: What must a property practitioner disclose to a buyer regarding their relationship with the seller?

options.A: Nothing — client confidentiality applies
options.B: Only if the buyer specifically requests this information
options.C: Only if the practitioner has a financial interest in the property
options.D: Any material facts that could affect the buyer's decision, including any dual mandate situation

questions.explanation: Full and timely disclosure of all material facts is a core obligation under the Code of Conduct.

questions.question: A practitioner is aware that the property is in a flood zone. They do not disclose this to the buyer. What are the consequences?

options.A: No consequences if the buyer did not specifically ask
options.B: The PPRA handles all flood-related disclosures
options.C: The seller alone bears responsibility for non-disclosure
options.D: The practitioner may face disciplinary action and potential legal liability for non-disclosure

questions.explanation: Known material facts that could affect a buyer's decision — including environmental risks — must be disclosed.

questions.question: A tenant's lease expires. The tenant continues to pay rent and the landlord continues to accept it. What legal principle applies?

options.A: The lease automatically renews for another full term
options.B: The tenant becomes a squatter with no rights
options.C: The lease continues on a month-to-month basis under tacit relocation
options.D: The landlord must give 3 months' notice to terminate

questions.explanation: Tacit relocation occurs when a lease expires but both parties continue acting as if it is still in force.

questions.question: What does the Consumer Protection Act (CPA) mean for property transactions?

options.A: It applies to all property sales including private sales between individuals
options.B: It requires all sale agreements to be in plain language
options.C: It exempts property practitioners from all liability for defects
options.D: It applies to property sold in the ordinary course of a developer's or agent's business, protecting buyers' rights

questions.explanation: The CPA applies where property is sold in the ordinary course of business — not to once-off private sales.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 'rates clearance certificate' in a property sale?

options.A: To confirm that the property has no outstanding building violations
options.B: To confirm the property's market value for transfer duty purposes
options.C: To confirm that all outstanding municipal rates, taxes, and levies have been paid
options.D: To confirm that the property complies with zoning regulations

questions.explanation: A rates clearance certificate confirms all rates and municipal accounts are up to date.

questions.question: Which BEST summarises the purpose of the Property Practitioners Act 22 of 2019?

options.A: To regulate the price of property in South Africa
options.B: To replace the Deeds Registries Act with a modern framework
options.C: To limit foreign ownership of South African property
options.D: To regulate property practitioners, protect consumers, and professionalise the property industry

questions.explanation: The Act's core objectives are professionalising the sector, protecting consumers, and ensuring ethical conduct.

questions.question: A practitioner operates as both a residential sales agent and a property manager. How many FFCs must they hold?

options.A: Two — one for each capacity
options.B: Three — one for each type of transaction
options.C: One — a single FFC covers all capacities in which a natural person acts
options.D: None — managing agents are exempt

questions.explanation: The Act provides that a single FFC is issued to a natural person regardless of how many capacities they operate in.

questions.question: The Property Practitioners Ombud's primary role is to:

options.A: Prosecute fraudulent property practitioners
options.B: Issue and revoke Fidelity Fund Certificates
options.C: Resolve disputes between property practitioners and consumers
options.D: Set commission rates for the industry

questions.explanation: The Ombud provides an accessible, cost-effective dispute resolution mechanism for consumers.

questions.question: A candidate property practitioner receives their FFC on 15 August 2024. Under which regulatory framework do they fall?

options.A: The Estate Agency Affairs Act 112 of 1976
options.B: The 2008 PPRA Education Regulations
options.C: The new Property Practitioners Act and the Occupational Certificate: Real Estate Agent (NQF4, SAQA ID 118714)
options.D: The National Qualifications Framework Act only

questions.explanation: All new FFCs issued from 1 July 2024 fall under the new regulatory framework.

questions.question: Which BEST describes the B-BBEE compliance requirement for property practitioners?

options.A: Only principal agents need a B-BBEE certificate
options.B: B-BBEE compliance is voluntary for sole proprietors
options.C: A valid B-BBEE certificate is required — without it, a practitioner may be disqualified from holding an FFC
options.D: B-BBEE compliance only applies to agencies with more than 50 employees

questions.explanation: The Property Practitioners Act introduced B-BBEE compliance as a new disqualification ground.

questions.question: A principal fails to renew their FFC before 31 October. On 1 November they conclude a sale. What is their legal position?

options.A: The sale is valid but they must renew their FFC within 30 days
options.B: They have a 30-day grace period provided they have applied for renewal
options.C: The sale is valid — FFC renewal is only required by 31 December
options.D: They are operating without a valid FFC and are not entitled to commission for that transaction

questions.explanation: There is no grace period. If the FFC has not been renewed by 31 October, the practitioner is unregistered.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOW included in the broader definition of 'property practitioner'?

options.A: Attorneys who sell property in the course of their legal practice
options.B: Banks that repossess and sell properties
options.C: Property managing agents and bond originators who earn commission
options.D: Municipal officials who sell council property

questions.explanation: The definition was significantly broadened to include managing agents and others previously unregulated.

questions.question: What is the function of the Property Practitioners Regulatory Authority Board?

options.A: To process FFC applications for individual practitioners
options.B: To conduct disciplinary hearings for all complaints
options.C: To govern and provide strategic oversight of the PPRA as an institution
options.D: To set property prices and commission caps

questions.explanation: The Board governs the PPRA at a strategic level; day-to-day operations are handled by executive management.

questions.question: A property practitioner is placed under provisional sequestration. What is the immediate consequence for their FFC?

options.A: Nothing changes until a final sequestration order is granted
options.B: The FFC is suspended pending the outcome
options.C: The practitioner may continue working if their principal approves
options.D: The practitioner is disqualified from holding an FFC — insolvency is a disqualification ground

questions.explanation: Insolvency (including provisional sequestration) is a ground for disqualification under the Act.

questions.question: Under the Act, who bears the cost of the mandatory disclosure form?

options.A: The property practitioner
options.B: The buyer
options.C: The PPRA
options.D: The seller or lessor — it is their obligation to complete and provide the form

questions.explanation: The obligation to complete the mandatory disclosure form rests with the seller or lessor.

questions.question: A practitioner's conviction is overturned on appeal. What happens to their FFC status?

options.A: It remains withdrawn permanently
options.B: The PPRA automatically reinstates the FFC
options.C: The practitioner may apply for reinstatement of their FFC following the successful appeal
options.D: A new FFC is automatically issued when the conviction is set aside

questions.explanation: A successful appeal removes the disqualification but reinstatement requires a formal application to the PPRA.

questions.question: A practitioner's FFC is valid. They conduct business under a different name to their registered details. Is this permissible?

options.A: Yes — the FFC is personal and not linked to the agency name
options.B: Yes — provided their principal is aware
options.C: No — the FFC is linked to their registration details and must be used correctly
options.D: Only if they operate as a sole proprietor

questions.explanation: The FFC is tied to the practitioner's registered details — operating under a different name is a compliance breach.

questions.question: How many times per year does the PPRA schedule Professional Designation Examinations?

options.A: Twice a year
options.B: Three times a year
options.C: Four times a year — generally February, May, August, and November
options.D: Six times a year

questions.explanation: The PPRA schedules approximately four PDE examination dates per year.

questions.question: A practitioner passes their PDE4 exam but does not apply for their FFC within the required period. What happens?

options.A: The PDE result remains valid indefinitely
options.B: The practitioner automatically qualifies as a full-status agent
options.C: The practitioner must still apply for and obtain their FFC before they may operate as a full-status agent
options.D: The PDE result expires after 6 months

questions.explanation: Passing the PDE4 is a prerequisite for full status but the practitioner must still obtain their FFC.

questions.question: A candidate completes their 12-month internship but fails their PDE4 exam. What must they do?

options.A: Repeat the full 12-month internship before being allowed to write again
options.B: Apply for a permanent exemption from the PDE4
options.C: Re-enrol for the PDE4 and write the exam again within the prescribed period
options.D: Submit a logbook to the PPRA in lieu of the PDE4

questions.explanation: Failing the PDE4 does not cancel the internship — the candidate must re-enrol and write again.

questions.question: A practitioner submits their FFC renewal on 29 October but the PPRA only processes it in November. Is the practitioner protected?

options.A: No — only the processing date matters, not the submission date
options.B: Only if the practitioner has proof of electronic submission
options.C: Yes — provided they submitted their application and payment before 31 October, they are generally protected
options.D: No — the practitioner must cease trading immediately on 1 November

questions.explanation: Timely submission before the deadline is the key requirement — processing delays should not penalise a compliant practitioner.

questions.question: Which of the following is a ground on which the PPRA may REFUSE to issue an FFC?

options.A: The applicant is under 25 years of age
options.B: The applicant has never previously held an FFC
options.C: The applicant lives in a different province to where they want to work
options.D: The applicant has been convicted of a fraudulent offence involving dishonesty

questions.explanation: A conviction for fraud or dishonesty is a disqualification ground under the Act.

questions.question: A non-principal wants to become a principal. What additional examination must they pass?

options.A: The PDE4 again
options.B: The SAICA Professional Competence Exam
options.C: The PDE5 (Professional Designation Examination, Level 5)
options.D: No additional exam — they only need additional experience

questions.explanation: The PDE5 is the examination required for principal status.

questions.question: A practitioner holds a valid FFC but receives a disqualification notice from the PPRA. What must they do?

options.A: Continue trading and challenge the notice in court
options.B: Contact their estate agency principal for guidance
options.C: Ignore the notice if they believe it was issued in error
options.D: Cease operating immediately and follow the PPRA's prescribed process to respond to the notice

questions.explanation: A disqualification notice must be taken seriously — the practitioner must cease operating and follow the formal process.

questions.question: For the purposes of the PDE4 examination, what type of exam format does the PPRA use?

options.A: Multiple choice only
options.B: Written essays only
options.C: Oral examination conducted by a PPRA examiner
options.D: An open-book examination testing practical application of knowledge

questions.explanation: The PDE4 is an open-book examination assessing practical application of knowledge.

questions.question: A practitioner loses their FFC certificate document. What must they do?

options.A: Contact the PPRA to issue a duplicate at no charge
options.B: Apply for a completely new FFC from scratch
options.C: Apply to the PPRA for a replacement FFC, following their prescribed process
options.D: Continue operating — the FFC status is on the system regardless

questions.explanation: Practitioners must apply to the PPRA for a replacement certificate.

questions.question: Agency A has a sole mandate. Agency B has an open mandate. Agency A introduces a buyer first. Before transfer the buyer signs a new offer with Agency B. Who is entitled to commission?

options.A: Agency B — they concluded the final valid offer
options.B: Both agencies share the commission equally
options.C: Neither agency — the seller caused the confusion
options.D: Agency A — they were the effective cause of the sale, having first introduced the buyer

questions.explanation: Effective cause determines commission entitlement — Agency A introduced the buyer first.

questions.question: A mandate specifies 5% commission. The seller verbally instructs a price reduction to R2 200 000. An offer at R2 200 000 is accepted. What is the commission basis?

options.A: 5% of the original R2 500 000 mandated price
options.B: 5% of R2 200 000 — the actual sale price
options.C: 5% of R2 350 000 — the average of both prices
options.D: 5% of R2 200 000, provided the agent agreed to the price reduction — ideally confirmed in writing

questions.explanation: If the agent agreed to the price reduction, commission is calculated on the actual sale price.

questions.question: A rental agreement is for 12 months at R10 000 per month. Commission is 10% of first month plus 5% thereafter. What is the total commission?
questions.explanation: First month: R10 000 x 10% = R1 000. Remaining 11 months: R10 000 x 5% x 11 = R5 500. Total = R6 500.

questions.question: A sole mandate expired. A new agent introduces a buyer who concludes a sale. Who gets the commission?

options.A: The original agent — they did most of the marketing work
options.B: Both agents share the commission
options.C: The original agent — their expired mandate still protects them
options.D: The new agent — the sole mandate expired and the effective cause of this sale was their introduction

questions.explanation: Once a sole mandate expires, the protection it offered lapses — the new agent is the effective cause.

questions.question: A practitioner receives a written sole mandate that does not specify a commission rate. What happens?

options.A: The standard industry rate of 7% applies automatically
options.B: The PPRA prescribes a fixed rate that applies
options.C: Commission is negotiable between the practitioner and the seller — there is no prescribed fixed rate in South Africa
options.D: No commission may be charged if it is not specified in the mandate

questions.explanation: There is no fixed commission rate prescribed by law in South Africa — commission is negotiable.

questions.question: A buyer signs an offer and pays a deposit. The seller then withdraws without just cause. What happens to the deposit?

options.A: The deposit is split equally between buyer and seller
options.B: The seller retains the deposit as a penalty
options.C: The agent retains the deposit as commission
options.D: The deposit must be refunded to the buyer — the seller's breach entitles the buyer to cancellation and a refund

questions.explanation: Where the seller is in breach, the buyer is entitled to cancel the agreement and claim a full refund.

questions.question: A written mandate is signed but not dated. What is the legal impact?

options.A: The mandate is automatically invalid
options.B: The mandate is valid for 30 days from the date of signing
options.C: The mandate may be enforceable but the absence of a date creates uncertainty
options.D: The PPRA must approve undated mandates before they are valid

questions.explanation: While an undated mandate is not automatically void, the absence of a date creates practical and legal difficulties.

questions.question: A commercial property is sold. Is this the same mandate process as a residential mandate?

options.A: No — commercial mandates must be approved by the PPRA before signing
options.B: No — commercial mandates require a separate commercial FFC
options.C: Yes — the same mandate principles and Act requirements apply
options.D: Yes — the same principles apply, but commercial transactions may have additional due diligence requirements

questions.explanation: The Act applies to all property transactions but commercial transactions may have more complex due diligence.

questions.question: A practitioner signs a mandate with the husband in a marriage in community of property. The wife did not sign. Is the mandate valid?

options.A: Yes — one spouse may act on behalf of both
options.B: Yes — only the registered owner needs to sign
options.C: No — in a marriage in community of property, both spouses must consent to the sale of immovable property
options.D: Only valid if the husband provides a signed affidavit on behalf of his wife

questions.explanation: In a marriage in community of property, both spouses must consent to and sign any mandate to sell.

questions.question: A practitioner believes a listed property is overpriced. What should they do?

options.A: Advertise at the seller's price — their instruction is final
options.B: Refuse the mandate immediately
options.C: Advertise at their own valuation without telling the seller
options.D: Advise the seller professionally of the risk, document this advice, and ultimately respect the seller's final decision

questions.explanation: The practitioner has a professional duty to advise honestly and document advice — then respect the seller's decision.

questions.question: A buyer pays a deposit directly into the seller's personal bank account. What is the risk?

options.A: None — the seller is the rightful recipient
options.B: Minor administrative inconvenience only
options.C: The agent loses their right to commission
options.D: The funds are not protected under the Fidelity Fund and the buyer has no recourse if the seller spends the money before transfer

questions.explanation: Deposits must be held in the agent's trust account to be protected under the Fidelity Fund.

questions.question: A practitioner operates with a PPRA trust account exemption. A buyer asks them to hold the deposit. What must they tell the buyer?

options.A: They may hold the deposit in their business account instead
options.B: They may hold the deposit in any account as long as it earns interest
options.C: They must inform the buyer that they cannot hold the deposit and an alternative must be arranged
options.D: They should accept the deposit and immediately transfer it to the PPRA

questions.explanation: A practitioner with a trust account exemption cannot hold client funds — full disclosure and alternatives required.

questions.question: A practitioner receives a deposit before the sale agreement is concluded. May they hold this in trust?

options.A: No — only deposits for concluded agreements may be held
options.B: Only if the seller gives written permission
options.C: Yes — trust accounts are designed to hold client funds at any stage of a property transaction
options.D: Only for a maximum of 48 hours before the sale must be concluded

questions.explanation: Trust accounts hold client funds at any stage — before, during, or after a transaction.

questions.question: A principal discovers their bookkeeper has been transferring amounts from trust to the business account. What must they do?

options.A: Reprimand the bookkeeper and correct the entries
options.B: Repay the amounts to trust and say nothing further
options.C: Report it to the auditor at year-end
options.D: Report it to the PPRA and the relevant authorities immediately — this constitutes misappropriation of trust funds

questions.explanation: Misappropriation of trust funds is a serious criminal offence that must be reported to the PPRA.

questions.question: A trust account shows a deficit — more money has been paid out than was received. What are the consequences?

options.A: A bookkeeping error with no serious consequences
options.B: A temporary cash flow issue that can be rectified next month
options.C: A trust account shortfall — this is a serious regulatory and criminal matter that must be reported to the PPRA
options.D: An acceptable situation if the agency is profitable overall

questions.explanation: A trust account shortfall means client money is missing — a critical breach requiring immediate reporting.

questions.question: Who is ultimately responsible for ensuring a property agency's trust account is properly maintained?

options.A: The bookkeeper
options.B: The external auditor
options.C: The PPRA's compliance department
options.D: The principal property practitioner

questions.explanation: The principal is ultimately responsible for all aspects of the agency's compliance including trust account management.

questions.question: A landlord instructs a managing agent to invest rental deposits. The managing agent keeps the interest. This is:

options.A: Acceptable — managing the investment account is a service that justifies the fee
options.B: Acceptable if agreed to verbally between the parties
options.C: Acceptable only if the amount is under R500 per month
options.D: A violation of the Act — interest must be paid to the party entitled to it

questions.explanation: The Act prohibits practitioners from soliciting or retaining interest on trust funds.

questions.question: A practitioner receives a deposit via EFT. When should this be reflected in the trust account records?

options.A: Within 7 days of receipt
options.B: Within 48 hours of receipt
options.C: At the end of the month during reconciliation
options.D: Immediately upon receipt — trust receipts must be recorded promptly and accurately

questions.explanation: Prompt and accurate recording of trust receipts is a basic requirement.

questions.question: A property practitioner closes their agency. They have R45 000 in rental deposits in trust. What must they do?

options.A: Donate the funds to a registered charity
options.B: Retain the funds in their personal account until the leases expire
options.C: Return the funds to the PPRA for distribution
options.D: Transfer the deposits to the incoming agency or to the landlords/tenants as directed — with proper documentation

questions.explanation: Trust funds belong to clients — when an agency closes all trust funds must be properly returned or transferred.

questions.question: A trust account audit finds that commission was deducted from trust before transfer was registered. Is this permissible?

options.A: Yes — the agent is entitled to their commission immediately upon conclusion of the sale
options.B: Yes — provided the seller has signed a written authorisation
options.C: Only if the seller has signed a specific written instruction authorising early release of commission from trust
options.D: No — commission may never be paid from trust funds

questions.explanation: Trust funds may only be released when lawfully instructed — early release of commission requires written authorisation.

questions.question: A practitioner values a friend's property then lists it. Should they accept the mandate?

options.A: No — there is an automatic conflict of interest when valuing and then selling
options.B: Only if they reduce their commission
options.C: Yes — provided they disclose their relationship to the seller and all parties are comfortable with the arrangement
options.D: Only with the PPRA's written approval

questions.explanation: Personal relationships require disclosure but do not automatically prevent a practitioner from taking a mandate.

questions.question: A practitioner witnesses a colleague submitting false documents to secure a sale. What is their ethical and legal obligation?

options.A: Stay out of it — it is not their transaction
options.B: Speak to the colleague privately and give them a chance to correct it
options.C: Report it only if the buyer suffers financial loss
options.D: Report the conduct to the principal and/or the PPRA — failing to report makes them complicit

questions.explanation: Property practitioners have a professional duty to report dishonest conduct.

questions.question: A practitioner pressures a buyer by falsely telling them another offer is imminent. This conduct is called:

options.A: Effective salesmanship
options.B: Urgency marketing
options.C: Misrepresentation — a serious breach of the Code of Conduct
options.D: Acceptable negotiation strategy

questions.explanation: Creating false urgency through misrepresentation is a clear ethical violation.

questions.question: What does 'arm's length transaction' mean in property sales?

options.A: A sale concluded without the use of an estate agent
options.B: A sale where both parties are represented by attorneys
options.C: A transaction where both parties are independent and neither has undue influence over the other
options.D: A sale where the parties have never met in person

questions.explanation: An arm's length transaction ensures that the sale price reflects true market value.

questions.question: A practitioner is offered a 50% ownership stake in a property they are marketing, in lieu of commission. Should they accept?

options.A: Yes — this is a legitimate alternative payment method
options.B: Yes, provided the offer is documented in writing
options.C: Only with the PPRA's approval
options.D: No — taking an ownership interest in a property they are marketing creates a serious conflict of interest

questions.explanation: Accepting ownership in a property you are marketing creates a direct conflict between your duty and your financial interest.

questions.question: A practitioner finds out their client is involved in money laundering through a property purchase. What must they do?

options.A: Continue with the transaction — it is not their responsibility
options.B: Withdraw from the transaction but keep it confidential
options.C: Confront the client directly and give them a chance to explain
options.D: Report the suspicious transaction to the Financial Intelligence Centre (FIC) as required by FICA

questions.explanation: Property practitioners are accountable institutions under FICA and legally obligated to report suspicious transactions.

questions.question: A practitioner receives a higher offer after already accepting an offer on behalf of their seller. What must they do?

options.A: Switch to the higher offer — the seller's best interest comes first
options.B: Ignore the higher offer — once an offer is accepted it cannot be undone
options.C: Accept the higher offer and pay the original buyer compensation
options.D: Present the higher offer to the seller and advise them properly — the seller makes the final decision guided by their legal obligations

questions.explanation: Once an offer is accepted a binding contract exists — the practitioner must advise the seller of both the new offer and existing legal obligations.

questions.question: A practitioner posts on social media about a sale without the seller's authorisation. What ethical principle has been violated?

options.A: The practitioner's right to market themselves
options.B: The buyer's right to privacy
options.C: The seller's right to confidentiality regarding their private transaction
options.D: The PPRA's marketing guidelines

questions.explanation: Disclosing details of a private transaction without the client's consent violates the duty of confidentiality.

questions.question: A practitioner receives a complaint from a buyer about the condition of a property after transfer. What should they do first?

options.A: Refer the buyer directly to an attorney
options.B: Deny all responsibility and refer to the voetstoots clause
options.C: Contact the seller and instruct them to deal with it
options.D: Listen to the complaint, assess the facts, and advise both parties appropriately — referring them to the PPRA Ombud if a resolution cannot be reached

questions.explanation: Practitioners have a professional duty to assist in resolving disputes.

questions.question: A practitioner retaliates against a buyer who submitted a complaint to the PPRA by refusing to return their calls. This behaviour:

options.A: Is acceptable — the complaint process takes care of itself
options.B: Only becomes an issue if the PPRA finds in the buyer's favour
options.C: Is acceptable if the complaint was found to be frivolous
options.D: Constitutes unprofessional conduct and a breach of the Code of Conduct

questions.explanation: Retaliating against a client for exercising their right to complain is a serious breach of professional standards.

questions.question: What is the legal effect of a 'resolutive condition' in a sale agreement?

options.A: It prevents the sale from becoming binding until the condition is met
options.B: It allows the seller to withdraw at any time before transfer
options.C: The sale is immediately binding but will fall away if the condition occurs
options.D: It requires the buyer to pay a deposit before the condition is triggered

questions.explanation: Unlike a suspensive condition, a resolutive condition makes the sale immediately binding but may terminate it on a future event.

questions.question: What is a 'latent defect' in property law?

options.A: A defect that is clearly visible during a routine inspection
options.B: A defect that was created by the previous owner
options.C: A defect that only appears after transfer of ownership
options.D: A defect that is hidden and not discoverable through ordinary inspection

questions.explanation: A latent defect is one that is hidden and cannot be discovered through a reasonable inspection.

questions.question: A seller deliberately conceals a crack in the foundation. The buyer discovers it after transfer. What is the seller's legal position under the voetstoots clause?

options.A: Fully protected — the voetstoots clause covers all defects
options.B: Partially protected — they pay only for the repair cost
options.C: Protected — the buyer should have done a building inspection
options.D: Not protected — deliberate concealment of a known latent defect removes the voetstoots protection

questions.explanation: Deliberate concealment of a known defect constitutes fraudulent misrepresentation and removes voetstoots protection.

questions.question: A property is sold subject to an existing lease agreement. What obligation does this create for the new owner?

options.A: The new owner may cancel the lease immediately upon transfer
options.B: The new owner must renegotiate the lease from scratch
options.C: The tenant must re-apply to the new owner to remain in the property
options.D: The new owner is bound by the existing lease — 'huur gaat voor koop' (lease goes before sale)

questions.explanation: The principle of 'huur gaat voor koop' means an existing lease survives a change of ownership.

questions.question: What is the Alienation of Land Act's main protective mechanism for instalment sale buyers?

options.A: It fixes the interest rate on instalment agreements
options.B: It requires all instalment sales to be approved by the PPRA
options.C: It prohibits instalment sale agreements entirely
options.D: It provides that buyers under instalment agreements cannot be evicted without proper legal process

questions.explanation: The Act protects buyers who pay for property in instalments before transfer.

questions.question: A property is described as 450 sqm. After transfer, the buyer discovers it is only 380 sqm. What legal remedy does the buyer have?

options.A: None — all property is sold voetstoots
options.B: They may request a refund of 10% of the purchase price
options.C: They must accept the property but can claim a rental reduction from the agent
options.D: They may have a claim for misrepresentation and may be entitled to a reduction in the purchase price or cancellation

questions.explanation: A significant discrepancy in the size of a property constitutes misrepresentation.

questions.question: When is stamp duty payable on a property transaction in South Africa?

options.A: Before the offer to purchase is signed
options.B: Within 6 months of the sale agreement being concluded
options.C: Immediately after the buyer's bond is approved
options.D: Stamp duty was abolished in South Africa — transfer duty applies instead

questions.explanation: Stamp duty on property transactions was abolished in South Africa in 2009.

questions.question: What is an 'antenuptial contract' and how does it affect a property sale?

options.A: A contract signed before a business partnership
options.B: A contract between an agent and buyer before a sale is concluded
options.C: A prenuptial agreement that specifies how assets including property are owned and dealt with during the marriage
options.D: A contract that must be registered with the PPRA before property is sold

questions.explanation: An ANC specifies the matrimonial property system — it determines whether one or both spouses must consent to property transactions.

questions.question: Which correctly describes the difference between a personal servitude and a praedial servitude?

options.A: A personal servitude lasts forever; a praedial servitude has a time limit
options.B: A personal servitude benefits a property; a praedial servitude benefits a person
options.C: Both types are identical — the names are used interchangeably
options.D: A personal servitude benefits a specific individual and ends when they die; a praedial servitude benefits a property and transfers with it

questions.explanation: Personal servitudes are tied to a specific person; praedial servitudes are tied to land and transfer with the property.

questions.question: A buyer makes an offer which is accepted. The next day the buyer changes their mind. What are the legal consequences?

options.A: The buyer may withdraw freely within 24 hours of signing
options.B: The buyer loses only their deposit
options.C: The seller must accept the withdrawal and find a new buyer
options.D: The buyer is in breach of a binding contract and the seller may claim damages or enforce the agreement

questions.explanation: Once both parties have signed an offer to purchase, a binding contract exists — the buyer cannot simply withdraw.

questions.question: Under FICA, property practitioners are classified as:

options.A: Exempt institutions — FICA only applies to banks
options.B: Reporting institutions with limited obligations
options.C: Accountable institutions with full FICA obligations including KYC and suspicious transaction reporting
options.D: Supervised institutions under the jurisdiction of the JSE

questions.explanation: Property practitioners are listed as accountable institutions under FICA.

questions.question: What does 'KYC' stand for in the context of FICA compliance?

options.A: Keep Your Contacts
options.B: Know Your Commission
options.C: Know Your Client
options.D: Key Yield Calculation

questions.explanation: KYC — Know Your Client — requires property practitioners to verify the identity of their clients before conducting business.

questions.question: A property practitioner accepts a cash payment of R50 000 from a buyer as a deposit. What must they do in terms of FICA?

options.A: Accept the cash — there is no restriction on cash payments
options.B: Refuse the cash and insist on an EFT
options.C: Accept the cash but do not issue a receipt
options.D: Report the cash transaction to the FIC as required — cash transactions above R49 999 must be reported

questions.explanation: Under FICA, cash transactions of R50 000 or more must be reported to the Financial Intelligence Centre.

questions.question: A client refuses to provide their identity document for FICA verification. What must the practitioner do?

options.A: Proceed with the transaction and document the client's refusal
options.B: Accept an alternative document such as a driver's licence instead
options.C: Notify the PPRA of the client's non-compliance
options.D: Decline to do business with the client — FICA requires identity verification before proceeding

questions.explanation: FICA is clear: if a client refuses to provide information required for identity verification, the institution may not proceed.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 'rates clearance certificate' in a property transfer?

options.A: To confirm zoning compliance of the property
options.B: To confirm no building plan violations exist
options.C: To confirm the property is registered in the seller's name
options.D: To confirm that all outstanding municipal rates, taxes, and levies have been paid up to a specified date

questions.explanation: A rates clearance certificate confirms all municipal accounts are settled — required before registration of transfer.
