questions.question: A practitioner notes that a property may have been built without approved building plans. What must they do?

options.A: Say nothing — building compliance is the seller's responsibility
options.B: Advise the buyer informally but do not put it in writing
options.C: Withdraw from the mandate immediately without explanation
options.D: Disclose the potential non-compliance to the buyer and advise the seller to regularise the plans — document all advice given

questions.explanation: Known potential compliance issues are material facts that must be disclosed.

questions.question: Which certificate confirms that a property's electrical installation complies with the Occupational Health and Safety Act?

options.A: The electrical rates certificate
options.B: The municipal compliance certificate
options.C: The Electrical Compliance Certificate (COC) issued by a registered electrician
options.D: The SABS compliance certificate

questions.explanation: An Electrical COC is required by law before a property is transferred.

questions.question: A buyer discovers 6 months after transfer that the property was used as a methamphetamine lab. Does the buyer have recourse?

options.A: No — the property was sold voetstoots
options.B: No — the estate agent is not liable for the previous owner's activities
options.C: No — the buyer should have commissioned a full inspection before purchase
options.D: Possibly yes — if the seller knew about the contamination and deliberately concealed it, the voetstoots clause would not protect them

questions.explanation: Deliberate concealment of known facts that materially affect a property's value removes voetstoots protection.

questions.question: The Consumer Protection Act (CPA) protects buyers in which type of property transaction?

options.A: All private sales between individuals
options.B: Only sales of properties valued under R1 million
options.C: Only sales of new developments off-plan
options.D: Sales conducted in the ordinary course of a developer's, agency's, or seller's business

questions.explanation: The CPA applies where property is sold in the ordinary course of business — not to once-off private sales between individuals.

questions.question: A property practitioner fails to comply with FICA requirements. What is the maximum criminal penalty they face?

options.A: A R5 000 fine
options.B: Suspension of their FFC for 6 months
options.C: A formal warning from the PPRA
options.D: A substantial fine and/or imprisonment — FICA non-compliance carries serious criminal penalties

questions.explanation: FICA carries significant penalties for non-compliance including criminal prosecution, fines, and imprisonment.

questions.question: Mrs Dlamini signs a sole mandate with Easy Realty on 1 March (running until 30 April). On 15 April, her neighbour introduces a buyer who purchases the property directly. Is Easy Realty entitled to commission?

options.A: No — the neighbour found the buyer, not Easy Realty
options.B: No — Mrs Dlamini has the right to sell privately at any time
options.C: Only 50% commission — the neighbour deserves some recognition
options.D: Yes — the sole mandate was active and Mrs Dlamini sold during the mandate period, entitling Easy Realty to commission

questions.explanation: A sole mandate means that during the mandate period, any sale — including one facilitated by the seller — entitles the agent to commission.

questions.question: Mr Patel completes his NQF4 qualification but fails his PDE4. What happens if he doesn't write again within the prescribed period?

options.A: He is automatically granted full status
options.B: He may apply for an indefinite extension from the PPRA
options.C: He retains candidate status permanently
options.D: He may lose his ability to upgrade to full status if he does not pass within the PPRA's prescribed timeframe

questions.explanation: The PPRA prescribes the period within which candidates must pass their PDE — failure to comply may affect ability to obtain full status.

questions.question: Agent Thabo introduces Buyer Sipho to Seller Nomsa's property. Sipho's offer is rejected. Six months later — after Thabo's mandate expired — Sipho buys directly from Nomsa. Is Thabo entitled to commission?

options.A: Yes — Thabo introduced the buyer, so he is always entitled
options.B: Yes — 50% commission as Nomsa completed the transaction herself
options.C: No — the mandate had expired and too much time had passed; effective cause needs to be re-assessed
options.D: No — rejected offers cancel all commission entitlement

questions.explanation: This is a grey area — effective cause is the key test and courts consider the time elapsed and the causal link.

questions.question: A practitioner discovers mid-transaction that the seller has outstanding SARS tax debt and a potential lien on the property. What must they do?

options.A: Continue the transaction — SARS issues are not their concern
options.B: Advise the buyer verbally but proceed with the transaction
options.C: Withdraw from the mandate without advising the buyer
options.D: Disclose the information to the buyer as a material fact and advise the seller to resolve the SARS matter before transfer

questions.explanation: Outstanding SARS debt and potential liens on property are material facts that must be disclosed.

questions.question: A landlord wants to claim from the Fidelity Fund after a tenant causes R80 000 damage and disappears. Is this possible?

options.A: Yes — all property-related losses are covered by the Fidelity Fund
options.B: Yes — the agent failed in their duty to screen the tenant
options.C: No — the Fidelity Fund covers losses caused by dishonest acts of property practitioners, not tenant damage
options.D: Yes — if the damage occurred while the property was under the agent's management

questions.explanation: The Fidelity Fund compensates for losses caused by dishonest or fraudulent conduct of property practitioners — not tenant behaviour.

questions.question: Agent Lee tells a buyer the property has a swimming pool, which it does not. The buyer purchases based partly on this representation. What is Agent Lee's potential liability?

options.A: None — the buyer should have inspected the property
options.B: None — verbal statements before signing are not binding
options.C: Limited — the buyer only gets a partial refund
options.D: Serious — Agent Lee may face a claim for misrepresentation, disciplinary action by the PPRA, and potential civil liability

questions.explanation: Misrepresenting a material feature of a property is fraudulent or negligent misrepresentation.

questions.question: A practitioner notices their principal has been using trust funds to pay agency salaries. The principal tells the agent to keep quiet or face dismissal. What must the agent do?

options.A: Keep quiet — the principal's authority must be respected
options.B: Resign immediately without reporting anything
options.C: Document the situation privately and wait to see if it is corrected
options.D: Report the matter to the PPRA and/or the relevant authorities — fear of dismissal does not excuse complicity in a criminal act

questions.explanation: Misappropriation of trust funds is a criminal offence — the agent has a legal and ethical obligation to report it.

questions.question: A couple purchases a property in their joint names. After divorce, one spouse wants to sell their half. What is the legal position?

options.A: Each spouse may sell their half independently
options.B: The spouse wanting to sell must apply to the PPRA for permission
options.C: The estate agent may assist with the partial sale immediately
options.D: Co-ownership means both parties must agree to any sale — one co-owner cannot sell their share without the other's agreement or a court order

questions.explanation: In co-ownership no co-owner can dispose of their share without the other's agreement or a court order.

questions.question: A developer sells 10 units off-plan. After 2 years, only 6 are completed. Buyers of the 4 uncompleted units want to cancel and get deposits back. What law primarily protects them?

options.A: The Property Practitioners Act
options.B: The Deeds Registries Act
options.C: The National Credit Act
options.D: The Housing Development Schemes for Retired Persons Act or the Alienation of Land Act, depending on the structure of the sale

questions.explanation: Off-plan purchases are governed by specific legislation providing protection for buyers who have paid before transfer.

questions.question: A practitioner is asked to sign as a witness on an offer to purchase. After signing, they realise the document contains an illegal clause. What must they do?

options.A: Nothing — as a witness, they bear no responsibility for the content
options.B: Strike through the clause themselves
options.C: Submit the document to the PPRA for review
options.D: Advise all parties of the illegal clause and recommend they obtain legal advice before proceeding

questions.explanation: A practitioner has a professional duty to highlight illegal clauses they are aware of.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of witnesses required on a sale agreement for immovable property to be valid?

options.A: None — witnesses are optional
options.B: Two witnesses on behalf of each party
options.C: One notary public
options.D: At least one witness per party who signs the agreement

questions.explanation: The Alienation of Land Act requires that each party's signature on a sale agreement be witnessed by at least one person.

questions.question: A rental agent fails to conduct a proper inspection before a new tenant moves in. The tenant later causes damage the landlord claims was pre-existing. What is the agent's position?

options.A: The tenant is automatically liable for all damage
options.B: The agent bears no responsibility — it is between the landlord and tenant
options.C: The agent is liable for the full cost of all damage
options.D: The agent may be found negligent for failing to conduct a proper entry inspection — a documented ingoing inspection protects all parties

questions.explanation: A proper ingoing inspection is essential — without it disputes about pre-existing damage cannot be resolved fairly.

questions.question: A buyer who is clearly intoxicated wants to sign the offer to purchase. What should the practitioner do?

options.A: Proceed — the buyer is an adult and responsible for their actions
options.B: Proceed but add a note to the file about the buyer's state
options.C: Ask the seller to sign later so the buyer sobers up first
options.D: Postpone the signing — a person who is intoxicated lacks the legal capacity to contract, and the agreement may be voidable

questions.explanation: Contractual capacity requires that a person be of sound mind at the time of contracting.

questions.question: A person claims to own a property but cannot produce the title deed. What should the practitioner do before accepting the mandate?

options.A: Accept the mandate based on the person's word and proceed
options.B: Ask a neighbour to confirm the person lives there
options.C: Accept the mandate but add a disclaimer to the marketing
options.D: Verify ownership through the Deeds Registry or with the conveyancer before accepting the mandate

questions.explanation: Verifying that the seller is the registered owner of the property is a basic due diligence step.

questions.question: A landlord instructs a property practitioner to discriminate against potential tenants based on race. What must the practitioner do?

options.A: Follow the instruction — the landlord's wishes must be respected
options.B: Follow the instruction but document their objection
options.C: Only follow the instruction if the landlord puts it in writing
options.D: Refuse to discriminate — racial discrimination is prohibited by the Constitution and PEPUDA

questions.explanation: Discrimination on the basis of race in the rental of property is unconstitutional and illegal.

questions.question: A practitioner markets a property as being in a 'prestigious security estate' when it is actually in a standard residential suburb. This is:

options.A: Acceptable marketing language that all buyers understand
options.B: Acceptable if the suburb has a low crime rate
options.C: Acceptable if the practitioner believes it to be prestigious
options.D: False advertising — a direct violation of the Code of Conduct and potentially the Consumer Protection Act

questions.explanation: Specific property descriptors like 'security estate' carry precise meanings — using them inaccurately is misrepresentation.

questions.question: A practitioner receives a signed offer on a Friday. The seller is unreachable until Monday. Is the offer still valid?

options.A: No — offers expire after 24 hours automatically
options.B: No — the offer must be countersigned within the same business day
options.C: Only if the offer includes a specific validity period and that period has not expired
options.D: Yes — provided the offer is still within its specified validity period when it is presented to the seller

questions.explanation: An offer remains valid for the period specified in the document.

questions.question: A property practitioner's agency is sold. The new owners want to terminate all existing mandates. What must they do?

options.A: Nothing — mandates automatically transfer with the business
options.B: Issue 24-hour notice to all clients
options.C: Only terminate mandates that have already expired
options.D: Provide proper written notice to all clients in accordance with the mandate terms — mandates cannot simply be cancelled without following the agreed process

questions.explanation: Mandates are legal agreements that cannot be unilaterally cancelled without following the cancellation provisions.

questions.question: A practitioner quotes a price based on emotions rather than comparable sales data. The property sells well below market value. This constitutes:

options.A: A reasonable professional opinion
options.B: Acceptable practice — valuations are always subjective
options.C: An isolated error with no professional consequences
options.D: Negligent valuation — a breach of the professional standard of care expected of a property practitioner

questions.explanation: Valuations must be based on factual comparable data and market analysis — emotion-based valuations are negligent.

questions.question: An agent concludes a rental agreement. The tenant pays 2 months' deposit plus first month's rent. All is deposited into trust. When may the agent release the first month's rent to the landlord?

options.A: Immediately — it belongs to the landlord
options.B: After 7 days
options.C: After the tenant has moved in and confirmed the condition of the property
options.D: When lawfully authorised to do so — typically after the lease commences and the agent is instructed to release the rental

questions.explanation: Even rental income held in trust must be released according to the agreement and proper authorisation.

questions.question: A buyer wants to purchase a property as a nominee for an undisclosed third party. What should the practitioner do?

options.A: Proceed — the buyer is the person signing the documents
options.B: Proceed — nominee purchases are common and legal
options.C: Decline immediately — nominee purchases are illegal
options.D: Proceed with caution — verify the identity of the actual beneficial owner as required under FICA and ensure full disclosure

questions.explanation: Nominee purchases trigger FICA obligations — the practitioner must identify the beneficial owner.

questions.question: After transfer, a buyer discovers the seller had an outstanding HOA levy debt of R35 000. Who is responsible?

options.A: The new owner — they bought the property as-is
options.B: The estate agent — they should have disclosed this
options.C: The conveyancer — they should have checked this
options.D: The seller — outstanding levies should have been disclosed and settled at transfer; the conveyancer is responsible for obtaining a levy clearance

questions.explanation: Outstanding HOA levies are the seller's responsibility — a levy clearance certificate is required before transfer.

questions.question: A rental agent fails to inspect a property at lease end and releases the deposit to the tenant. The landlord discovers significant damage. What is the agent's potential liability?

options.A: None — it is the landlord's responsibility to inspect their own property
options.B: The agent returns their commission as compensation
options.C: Limited — the agent is only liable for the cost of the inspection
options.D: Significant — the agent's failure to conduct an outgoing inspection and wrongful release of the deposit may expose them to liability

questions.explanation: A proper outgoing inspection is a fundamental duty of a rental agent — releasing the deposit without it is negligent.

questions.question: A property practitioner is given a mandate to sell a property in a deceased estate. The executor signs the mandate. Is this valid?

options.A: No — only the heirs may mandate the sale of estate property
options.B: No — the PPRA must approve all estate property sales
options.C: Only if all heirs sign simultaneously
options.D: Yes — the executor is the legal representative of the estate and has the authority to mandate the sale of estate assets

questions.explanation: An executor appointed by the Master of the High Court has full authority to administer and sell estate assets.

questions.question: What does the Latin term 'caveat emptor' mean in the context of property sales?

options.A: The buyer always wins in a dispute
options.B: The seller must disclose all defects
options.C: The agent bears responsibility for all errors
options.D: 'Let the buyer beware' — the buyer is responsible for satisfying themselves about the condition of the property

questions.explanation: Caveat emptor places the onus on the buyer to investigate the property before purchase.

questions.question: A practitioner markets a property using photos from 3 years ago when the garden was beautifully maintained. Currently the garden is severely neglected. This constitutes:

options.A: Acceptable practice — it shows the property's potential
options.B: Acceptable if the photos are accurately dated
options.C: Acceptable if the seller approved the photos
options.D: Misleading advertising — photos must accurately represent the current condition of the property

questions.explanation: Using outdated photos that misrepresent the current condition of a property is misleading and a breach of the Code of Conduct.

questions.question: A buyer's bond application is declined. The sale was subject to a bond approval condition. What happens to the deposit?

options.A: The seller keeps the deposit as a penalty for the failed bond
options.B: The agent deducts their commission before returning the deposit
options.C: The deposit is split between buyer and seller
options.D: The full deposit must be refunded to the buyer — the suspensive condition was not met and the agreement falls away

questions.explanation: When a suspensive condition fails, the agreement lapses and the deposit must be returned to the buyer in full.

questions.question: A practitioner's client base consists mostly of cash buyers. Must the practitioner still comply with FICA identity verification?

options.A: No — FICA only applies to financed transactions
options.B: No — FICA only applies to transactions above R5 million
options.C: Only for cash transactions above R50 000
options.D: Yes — FICA applies to all property transactions regardless of the payment method

questions.explanation: FICA applies to all property transactions — cash transactions require more scrutiny, not less.

questions.question: A conveyancer asks an agent to confirm their bank account details via an unfamiliar email address. What must the agent do?

options.A: Respond to the email immediately to avoid delaying settlement
options.B: Ask their principal to deal with the request
options.C: Provide the details — conveyancers always request this information
options.D: Verify the request by calling the conveyancer directly on a known number — this is a classic Business Email Compromise (BEC) fraud tactic

questions.explanation: Property BEC fraud involves criminals substituting fraudulent banking details — always verify verbally on a known number.

questions.question: A practitioner has completed this practice set. According to best study practice for the PDE4, what should they do next?

options.A: Book the exam immediately — 200 questions is enough preparation
options.B: Re-read the entire study guide from cover to cover
options.C: Focus only on the sections where they scored below 60%
options.D: Review all incorrect answers, understand why each answer was wrong, and then practise under timed conditions using the full PPRA study guide

questions.explanation: The PDE4 rewards practical application — understanding why answers are correct is key to open-book exam success.

questions.question: A landlord wants to increase a tenant's monthly rental by 15% mid-lease without an escalation clause. Is this permissible?

options.A: Yes — a landlord may increase rent at any time with 30 days' notice
options.B: Yes — inflation justifies any reasonable rent increase
options.C: Yes — provided the tenant agrees in writing
options.D: No — rent may only be increased in accordance with the terms of the lease agreement

questions.explanation: The lease agreement is a binding contract — a landlord cannot unilaterally increase rent outside the agreed terms.

questions.question: The Rental Housing Act applies to which type of rental property?

options.A: Commercial and industrial property only
options.B: All property, including commercial, industrial, and residential
options.C: Residential dwellings only
options.D: Only properties valued below R1 million

questions.explanation: The Rental Housing Act 50 of 1999 applies specifically to the rental of residential dwellings.

questions.question: Under the Rental Housing Act, what is the maximum rental deposit a landlord may charge?

options.A: One month's rent
options.B: Three months' rent
options.C: Two months' rent
options.D: There is no maximum — it is freely negotiable

questions.explanation: The Rental Housing Act caps the rental deposit at a maximum of two months' rent.

questions.question: A landlord fails to refund the deposit within the prescribed period. What is the landlord's liability?

options.A: The landlord must refund the deposit with 10% interest
options.B: The landlord may retain the deposit if they believe there is damage
options.C: The landlord must refund the deposit plus interest within the prescribed period or the tenant may approach the Rental Housing Tribunal
options.D: The landlord has 30 days to conduct repairs before refunding

questions.explanation: The Rental Housing Act prescribes strict timelines for deposit refunds — failure entitles the tenant to approach the Rental Housing Tribunal.

questions.question: A joint inspection of a rental property must be conducted at which two points?

options.A: Before advertising and after vacating
options.B: At the start of the lease (ingoing) and at the end of the lease (outgoing)
options.C: At the midpoint and end of the lease only
options.D: Only at the end of the lease when damage is suspected

questions.explanation: The Rental Housing Act requires both an ingoing inspection and an outgoing inspection.

questions.question: A landlord refuses to conduct an outgoing inspection. What happens to the deposit?

options.A: The tenant forfeits the deposit
options.B: The deposit is held in trust until a court order is obtained
options.C: The landlord may deduct damages without an inspection report
options.D: The landlord loses the right to make any deductions from the deposit and must refund it in full

questions.explanation: If the landlord fails to conduct or offer the outgoing inspection, they forfeit their right to make any deductions.

questions.question: A tenant has a month-to-month lease. How much notice must the landlord give to terminate?

options.A: 14 days
options.B: 30 days
options.C: One calendar month's written notice
options.D: 60 days

questions.explanation: For month-to-month leases, one calendar month's written notice is required by either party.

questions.question: The Consumer Protection Act entitles a tenant who signs a fixed-term residential lease to cancel before expiry. What is the notice period required?

options.A: 14 days
options.B: 30 days
options.C: 20 business days' written notice
options.D: 60 calendar days

questions.explanation: Under section 14 of the CPA, a tenant in a fixed-term lease may cancel with 20 business days' written notice.

questions.question: A property practitioner managing a rental property fails to pay the landlord rental income for three consecutive months, despite receiving it from the tenant. What offence has been committed?

options.A: Breach of mandate only
options.B: A civil dispute — no criminal element
options.C: Negligence — the practitioner may be sued but not criminally charged
options.D: Theft and misappropriation of trust funds — this is a criminal offence

questions.explanation: Rental income collected on behalf of a landlord belongs to the landlord — withholding it constitutes theft.

questions.question: A tenant sublets the property without the landlord's written consent. What is the legal position?

options.A: The subletting is valid as long as the original tenant still pays rent
options.B: The subtenant has full rights as a tenant
options.C: The landlord must accept the subtenant
options.D: The subletting is a breach of the lease — the landlord may take action to cancel the lease and evict

questions.explanation: Subletting without the landlord's prior written consent is a material breach of the lease agreement.

questions.question: What does the PIE Act protect?

options.A: Landlords against non-paying tenants
options.B: Property practitioners against fraudulent mandates
options.C: Unlawful occupants and tenants against eviction without a court order
options.D: Municipalities against illegal land invasions only

questions.explanation: The PIE Act prevents any person from being evicted from residential property without a court order.

questions.question: A tenant stops paying rent. The landlord changes the locks and removes the tenant's belongings. What has the landlord done?

options.A: Exercised a lawful right to protect their property
options.B: Performed a legitimate act of self-help
options.C: Committed an unlawful act — this constitutes an illegal eviction under the PIE Act
options.D: Taken a lawful but excessive step

questions.explanation: Changing locks or removing belongings without a court order is an illegal eviction regardless of the tenant's breach.

questions.question: When should a property practitioner invest a rental deposit received in an interest-bearing account?

options.A: Within 7 days of receipt
options.B: At the end of the month
options.C: Immediately or as soon as reasonably possible
options.D: Only if the lease is for longer than 12 months

questions.explanation: The Rental Housing Act requires that rental deposits be invested in an interest-bearing account as soon as possible.

questions.question: A lease agreement contains a clause allowing the landlord to enter the property at any time without notice. Is this clause valid?

options.A: Yes — landlords have the right to inspect their own property
options.B: Yes — provided the tenant agreed to it in writing
options.C: No — a tenant has the right to undisturbed enjoyment; reasonable notice is always required
options.D: Yes — but only during business hours

questions.explanation: A tenant's right to undisturbed enjoyment (vacua possessio) is fundamental — unrestricted entry clauses are generally unenforceable.

questions.question: What is the function of the Rental Housing Tribunal?

options.A: To register all lease agreements in South Africa
options.B: To set maximum rental rates per province
options.C: To resolve disputes between landlords and tenants quickly and cost-effectively without court proceedings
options.D: To license property managing agents

questions.explanation: The Rental Housing Tribunal provides free dispute resolution for landlord-tenant disputes — its rulings are binding.

questions.question: A tenant causes damage to a rental property that exceeds the value of the deposit. Who is responsible for the shortfall?

options.A: The managing agent
options.B: The Property Practitioners Fidelity Fund
options.C: The landlord must absorb the loss
options.D: The tenant — the landlord may sue the tenant for the balance over and above the deposit

questions.explanation: The deposit is a limited security — where damages exceed it, the landlord may institute a civil claim against the tenant.

questions.question: A fixed-term lease expires and the tenant refuses to vacate. What is this called?

options.A: Tacit relocation
options.B: Unlawful subletting
options.C: Lease novation
options.D: Holding over — the tenant becomes an unlawful occupant and the landlord must apply to court for an eviction order

questions.explanation: A tenant who refuses to vacate after the lease ends is 'holding over' and becomes an unlawful occupant.

questions.question: A landlord instructs a managing agent to keep a deposit for a tenant who vacated with no damage. What must the agent do?

options.A: Follow the landlord's instruction — they are the client
options.B: Keep the deposit and document the instruction
options.C: Comply if the landlord provides written authorisation
options.D: Refuse — the deposit belongs to the tenant and must be refunded; following the instruction would make the agent complicit in theft

questions.explanation: The agent's duty is to the law, not just the client — a deposit must be returned if no valid deductions exist.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a lease renewal clause?

options.A: It allows the landlord to cancel the lease early
options.B: It reduces the rental rate for loyal tenants
options.C: It gives the tenant the right or option to renew the lease at the end of the initial term, usually on specified terms
options.D: It automatically converts a fixed lease to month-to-month

questions.explanation: A renewal clause sets out the tenant's right to extend the lease and typically the escalation that will apply.

questions.question: A lease states rental will escalate by 8% per annum on 1 March. The tenant pays the increased rental on 1 April. Are they in breach?

options.A: No — the escalation only applies from the date of payment
options.B: No — a 30-day grace period always applies
options.C: Yes — the escalation took effect on 1 March as per the lease; late payment of the increased amount constitutes a breach
options.D: No — the landlord must issue a separate notice of the escalation first

questions.explanation: Where the escalation date is clearly stated in the lease, the new rental is automatically due on that date.

questions.question: A managing agent charges a tenant an administration fee not stated in the lease. Is this permissible?

options.A: Yes — administration fees are standard industry practice
options.B: Yes — provided the fee is reasonable
options.C: No — any fees charged to a tenant must be disclosed and agreed to in the lease agreement
options.D: Yes — the agent is entitled to a reasonable fee for their services

questions.explanation: Any fees charged must be authorised in the lease agreement — undisclosed fees constitute an unlawful charge.

questions.question: A tenant vacates and leaves behind furniture and personal belongings. What must the landlord do?

options.A: Dispose of the items immediately — the tenant has abandoned them
options.B: Sell the items to recover any rental arrears
options.C: Store the items safely and notify the tenant — the landlord may not dispose of them without following the correct legal process
options.D: Hand the items over to the SAPS

questions.explanation: A landlord may not simply dispose of abandoned property — they must notify the tenant and allow a reasonable period for collection.

questions.question: What does 'vacua possessio' mean in the context of a lease?

options.A: The tenant's obligation to keep the property clean
options.B: The landlord's right to inspect the property
options.C: The tenant's right to peaceful and undisturbed occupation of the property
options.D: The landlord's right to vacant possession on expiry of the lease

questions.explanation: Vacua possessio is a fundamental obligation — the landlord must ensure the tenant's undisturbed occupation.

questions.question: A tenant installs a satellite dish without the landlord's consent. At the end of the lease, the landlord demands it be removed and the wall restored. Is this a valid demand?

options.A: No — the dish adds value to the property
options.B: No — a satellite dish is a standard household fixture
options.C: Yes — improvements or alterations made without written consent must generally be removed and the property restored
options.D: Only if the wall was structurally damaged

questions.explanation: Alterations without the landlord's written consent may need to be reversed on vacation.

questions.question: A tenant breaches the lease by failing to pay rent. What notice must the landlord give before cancelling?

options.A: 7 days
options.B: 24 hours in cases of non-payment
options.C: 30 days regardless of the breach
options.D: The landlord must give written notice of the breach and allow a reasonable period to remedy it before cancellation

questions.explanation: Before cancelling a residential lease, the landlord must give the tenant notice and an opportunity to remedy the breach.

questions.question: What legislation primarily governs sectional title schemes?

options.A: The Property Practitioners Act 22 of 2019
options.B: The Deeds Registries Act 47 of 1937
options.C: The Sectional Titles Act 95 of 1986 and the Sectional Titles Schemes Management Act 8 of 2011
options.D: The Housing Act 107 of 1997

questions.explanation: Sectional title schemes are governed by the Sectional Titles Act and the STSMA.

questions.question: In a sectional title scheme, who owns the common property?

options.A: The developer until all units are sold
options.B: The body corporate as a legal entity
options.C: The managing agent on behalf of all owners
options.D: All unit owners jointly, in undivided shares proportional to their participation quota

questions.explanation: Common property is owned jointly by all unit owners in undivided shares.

questions.question: What is a 'participation quota' (PQ) in a sectional title scheme?

options.A: The number of votes an owner has at a body corporate meeting
options.B: The percentage of the levy each owner must pay
options.C: A fraction expressing the floor area of a section relative to the total floor area — used to calculate levy contributions and voting rights
options.D: The owner's share in the net asset value of the scheme

questions.explanation: The participation quota is calculated by dividing the floor area of a section by the total floor area of all sections.

questions.question: A body corporate levies a special levy to fund emergency roof repairs. An owner refuses to pay. What can the body corporate do?

options.A: Cancel the owner's access to common facilities only
options.B: Nothing — special levies are not legally binding
options.C: Apply to the Community Schemes Ombud Service (CSOS)
options.D: The body corporate may take legal action to recover the outstanding levy — unpaid levies are enforceable in court

questions.explanation: Levies, including special levies, are legally enforceable debts.

questions.question: A buyer purchases a sectional title unit. After transfer, they discover outstanding levy arrears from the previous owner. Who is responsible?

options.A: The new owner — they purchased the property as-is
options.B: The previous owner remains fully liable
options.C: The body corporate must write off the arrears
options.D: The new owner may be held responsible — outstanding levies form a real right against the property, which is why a levy clearance certificate is critical

questions.explanation: Outstanding levies are a real right against the property — buyers must obtain a levy clearance certificate before transfer.

questions.question: What is the Community Schemes Ombud Service (CSOS)?

options.A: A government body that manages all body corporates directly
options.B: A private company that mediates sectional title disputes for a fee
options.C: A statutory body that regulates community schemes and resolves disputes between owners and their schemes
options.D: A branch of the Master of the High Court dealing with property disputes

questions.explanation: The CSOS was established under the Community Schemes Ombud Service Act 9 of 2011.

questions.question: An owner in a sectional title scheme wants to renovate their unit by breaking through a load-bearing wall. What must they do?

options.A: Inform the body corporate after the renovation
options.B: Obtain a building plan approval from the municipality only
options.C: Nothing — owners may alter their own section freely
options.D: Obtain prior written approval from the body corporate AND municipal building plan approval

questions.explanation: Structural alterations in a sectional title unit require both body corporate approval and municipal building plan approval.

questions.question: In a sectional title scheme, what is 'common property' typically comprised of?

options.A: The interior walls and fixtures of each unit
options.B: Only the garden and swimming pool
options.C: The developer's unsold units
options.D: All parts of the land and building not forming part of any section — including passages, lifts, gardens, pools, parking areas, and exterior walls

questions.explanation: Common property includes everything that is not part of a privately owned section.

questions.question: A body corporate passes a rule that no pets are allowed in the scheme. An existing owner has a dog. Does the rule apply to them?

options.A: No — existing owners are always exempt from new rules
options.B: No — pet ownership is a constitutional right
options.C: Yes — body corporate rules approved by the required majority are binding on all owners, including existing ones
options.D: Only if the owner agrees to the new rule in writing

questions.explanation: Conduct rules approved by the body corporate are binding on all owners and occupants.

questions.question: What is a homeowners' association (HOA)?

options.A: A tenant's committee that manages a rental complex
options.B: A voluntary group of property owners in a street
options.C: A legal entity that manages a residential estate to which all property owners in the development must belong as a condition of ownership
options.D: A government body that oversees housing development standards

questions.explanation: An HOA is typically a non-profit company incorporated into the title conditions of a residential estate — membership is compulsory.

questions.question: An HOA imposes a fine on an owner for leaving their bins out. The owner refuses to pay. What are the HOA's options?

options.A: The HOA may not fine residents — only municipalities can do this
options.B: The HOA may forcibly remove the bins as a penalty
options.C: The HOA has no legal recourse other than expelling the member
options.D: The HOA may enforce the fine through the CSOS or civil court, as the fine is authorised by the HOA's constitution

questions.explanation: HOA rules and fines are contractually binding — non-compliance can be enforced through the CSOS or civil court.

questions.question: A property in an HOA estate is sold. The buyer is unaware of the HOA levy obligations. What is their position after transfer?

options.A: The buyer may elect not to join the HOA
options.B: The seller remains responsible for all HOA obligations
options.C: The buyer may negotiate with the HOA to reduce their levies
options.D: The buyer is automatically bound by the HOA conditions — membership and levy obligations are registered conditions of title

questions.explanation: HOA membership conditions are registered in the title deed — they transfer automatically to every new owner.

questions.question: What is a 'special resolution' in a sectional title body corporate?

options.A: A resolution passed by a simple majority of 50% plus 1
options.B: A resolution that requires unanimous consent from all owners
options.C: A resolution that requires at least 75% of the value of votes AND 75% of the number of votes of members present
options.D: A resolution that requires approval from the CSOS

questions.explanation: A special resolution in sectional title requires a 75% majority by both value and number.

questions.question: A sectional title unit owner wants to install solar panels on the roof. The roof is common property. What must they do?

options.A: Install the panels — owners have the right to use common property
options.B: Apply to the municipality for approval only
options.C: Apply to the body corporate for exclusive use rights or a special resolution approving the installation
options.D: Nothing — solar installations are permitted by law regardless of body corporate rules

questions.explanation: The roof is common property — any owner wishing to use it exclusively must obtain body corporate approval.

questions.question: A managing agent collects levies on behalf of a body corporate. These funds must be:

options.A: Deposited into the managing agent's business account for convenience
options.B: Held in the managing agent's personal trust account
options.C: Deposited into the body corporate's own bank account — the managing agent is not permitted to commingle body corporate funds
options.D: Invested in a money market account in the managing agent's name

questions.explanation: Levy funds belong to the body corporate — they must be deposited directly into the body corporate's own account.

questions.question: What does 'exclusive use area' mean in a sectional title scheme?

options.A: An area of common property that only the developer may access
options.B: A section of a unit that has been converted for private use
options.C: A portion of common property that is allocated for the exclusive use of a specific owner, such as a parking bay or garden
options.D: A section that has been subdivided from the original unit

questions.explanation: Exclusive use areas are portions of common property allocated exclusively to a specific owner.

questions.question: In a sectional title sale, who is responsible for ensuring the levy clearance certificate is obtained?

options.A: The seller must obtain it and provide it to the buyer
options.B: The body corporate is responsible for issuing it without being asked
options.C: The estate agent obtains it as part of the marketing process
options.D: The conveyancer must obtain the levy clearance certificate from the body corporate before transfer can be registered

questions.explanation: The conveyancer handling the transfer is responsible for obtaining the levy clearance certificate.

questions.question: What is the reserve fund in a sectional title scheme?

options.A: A contingency fund used for emergency costs when levies are unpaid
options.B: The managing agent's fee held in reserve
options.C: A fund set aside for future maintenance and repair of common property, required by law to be maintained by every body corporate
options.D: Funds held for potential legal costs against defaulting owners

questions.explanation: The STSMA requires all bodies corporate to maintain a reserve fund for long-term maintenance and repairs.

questions.question: An owner in a sectional title scheme is in arrears with their levies. What is the correct process for the body corporate?

options.A: Remove the owner's access to common facilities immediately
options.B: Issue a final notice and then cancel the owner's sectional title
options.C: Apply to the CSOS to confiscate the owner's unit
options.D: Issue a formal demand for payment, and if unpaid, institute legal proceedings to obtain a judgment

questions.explanation: The body corporate's legal remedy for levy arrears is to obtain a court judgment and enforce it.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a sectional title scheme and a freehold property?

options.A: Sectional title properties cannot be bonded
options.B: Freehold owners must pay levies; sectional title owners do not
options.C: In a freehold property, the owner owns the land and building outright; in sectional title, the owner owns a defined section and shares ownership of common property
options.D: Sectional title properties are always smaller than freehold properties

questions.explanation: A freehold owner has full ownership of both land and structure; a sectional title owner owns their section and shares common property.

questions.question: What is transfer duty and who pays it?

options.A: A tax paid by the seller on the profit from the sale
options.B: A municipal tax paid annually on the value of property
options.C: A tax levied by SARS on the buyer based on the purchase price of immovable property
options.D: A fee paid to the Deeds Office for registering the transfer

questions.explanation: Transfer duty is a SARS tax payable by the buyer when acquiring immovable property.

questions.question: Which property transaction is EXEMPT from transfer duty?

options.A: A property sold between family members below market value
options.B: Any property purchased for less than R500 000
options.C: A property purchased from a VAT-registered developer where VAT is included in the purchase price
options.D: A property donated between spouses married in community of property

questions.explanation: When purchasing from a VAT-registered developer, VAT replaces transfer duty — the two taxes are mutually exclusive.

questions.question: What is Capital Gains Tax (CGT) in the context of a property sale?

options.A: A tax on the rental income earned from a property
options.B: A tax on the transfer duty paid
options.C: A tax on the profit (capital gain) made when a property is sold for more than its base cost
options.D: A tax on the value of property at death

questions.explanation: CGT is levied on the gain realised when an asset including property is sold for more than its base cost.

questions.question: A married couple's primary residence is sold. What CGT exclusion applies?

options.A: R300 000 primary residence exclusion
options.B: R1 million exclusion for all properties
options.C: R2 million primary residence exclusion on the capital gain
options.D: No exclusion — CGT applies in full to all residential property sales

questions.explanation: South Africa provides a R2 million primary residence exclusion on capital gains from the sale of a primary residence.

questions.question: A property is sold for R3 500 000. The seller purchased it for R2 000 000. The capital gain is R1 500 000. After applying the R2 million primary residence exclusion, how much is subject to CGT?

options.C: R0 — the entire gain of R1 500 000 falls within the R2 million exclusion

questions.explanation: The capital gain of R1 500 000 is less than the R2 million primary residence exclusion — therefore R0 is subject to CGT.

questions.question: Who is responsible for calculating and paying CGT on a property sale?

options.A: The conveyancer on behalf of the seller
options.B: The estate agent as the facilitating party
options.C: SARS calculates and demands payment directly
options.D: The seller — CGT is declared in the seller's annual income tax return

questions.explanation: CGT is the seller's tax liability — it is calculated and declared by the seller in their annual income tax return.

questions.question: A non-resident sells immovable property in South Africa. What withholding obligation applies?

options.A: No tax applies to foreign sellers
options.B: The buyer must pay an additional 15% tax directly to SARS
options.C: The buyer must withhold a prescribed percentage of the purchase price and pay it to SARS on behalf of the non-resident seller
options.D: The non-resident seller must register for South African VAT before the sale

questions.explanation: The buyer must withhold a percentage and pay it to SARS — this pre-pays the non-resident's potential CGT liability.

questions.question: For what period must transfer duty be paid to SARS before the Deeds Office will register a property transfer?

options.A: Within 30 days of registration
options.B: Before the offer to purchase is signed
options.C: Within 6 months of the sale agreement
options.D: Transfer duty must be paid before registration — SARS issues a receipt that the Deeds Office requires

questions.explanation: Transfer duty must be paid to SARS and a receipt obtained before the Deeds Office will process the transfer.

questions.question: How is transfer duty calculated in South Africa?

options.A: A flat rate of 8% on the full purchase price
options.B: It is always capped at R150 000
options.C: Properties below R1 000 000 are exempt; above that a sliding scale applies
options.D: Transfer duty is calculated on a sliding scale with a threshold below which no duty is payable, and increasing rates applied to portions of the price above certain values

questions.explanation: South Africa uses a progressive transfer duty scale — a threshold below which no duty applies with increasing rates above.

questions.question: A developer sells a property and charges VAT. The buyer asks whether they also owe transfer duty. What is the correct answer?

options.A: Yes — both VAT and transfer duty are always payable
options.B: Transfer duty is reduced by 50% when VAT applies
options.C: Transfer duty applies only if the property exceeds R3 million
options.D: No — transfer duty is not payable on a transaction where VAT is charged; the two taxes are mutually exclusive

questions.explanation: VAT and transfer duty are mutually exclusive — where a VAT-registered vendor sells property in the course of their enterprise, VAT applies and transfer duty does not.

questions.question: What is 'rates' in the context of property ownership?

options.A: The interest rate charged on a home loan
options.B: A fee paid to the Deeds Office for title deed registration
options.C: A municipal tax levied on property owners based on the value of their property, used to fund local government services
options.D: A levy charged by the body corporate for scheme maintenance

questions.explanation: Rates (municipal property rates) are levied by local municipalities based on the market value of the property.

questions.question: Can SARS place a lien on immovable property to recover outstanding tax debts?

options.A: No — SARS can only attach personal assets, not immovable property
options.B: No — the buyer is protected by their title deed
options.C: Only if the property is commercial
options.D: Yes — SARS has the power to attach immovable property to recover outstanding tax debts, which is why a SARS compliance check is important before transfer

questions.explanation: SARS has broad recovery powers under the Tax Administration Act including the right to attach immovable property.

questions.question: What is the 'base cost' of a property for CGT purposes?

options.A: The municipal valuation of the property at purchase
options.B: The current market value of the property
options.C: The original purchase price plus the cost of improvements and qualifying expenses incurred
options.D: The transfer duty paid when the property was acquired

questions.explanation: The base cost includes the original purchase price, improvements, legal costs, and other qualifying costs.

questions.question: What is an 'estate agent's commission' treated as for income tax purposes?

options.A: A capital receipt not subject to income tax
options.B: A VAT-exempt supply of services
options.C: Income — commission earned is subject to income tax and VAT if the practitioner is registered for VAT
options.D: A deductible expense for the buyer

questions.explanation: Commission earned by a property practitioner is trading income subject to income tax and VAT if applicable.

questions.question: A buyer's bond is for R1 800 000. The bank requires a deposit of R200 000. What transfer duty applies to the full property purchase price of R2 000 000?

options.A: Transfer duty is calculated on the bond amount of R1 800 000
options.B: Transfer duty is calculated on the deposit amount of R200 000
options.C: No transfer duty applies as the buyer has a bank-approved bond
options.D: Transfer duty is calculated on the full purchase price of R2 000 000, regardless of the bond amount or deposit

questions.explanation: Transfer duty is based on the purchase price — not the bond amount. The source of the buyer's financing is irrelevant.

questions.question: Under the Property Practitioners Act, bond originators who earn commission from placing home loans are classified as:

options.A: Exempt from registration as they are regulated by the NCR
options.B: Financial advisors under the FAIS Act
options.C: Property practitioners — they must register with the PPRA and hold a valid FFC
options.D: Credit providers under the National Credit Act

questions.explanation: Bond originators who earn referral or placement fees fall within the definition of 'property practitioner.'

questions.question: What is the primary legislation that regulates the granting of home loans in South Africa?

options.A: The Property Practitioners Act 22 of 2019
options.B: The Banks Act 94 of 1990
options.C: The Deeds Registries Act 47 of 1937
options.D: The National Credit Act 34 of 2005 (NCA)

questions.explanation: The NCA governs all credit agreements in South Africa including home loans.

questions.question: What does 'affordability assessment' mean under the National Credit Act?

options.A: An assessment of the property's market value by a registered valuer
options.B: The bank's review of the property's rental income potential
options.C: A mandatory assessment by the credit provider to determine whether the applicant can afford the credit repayments without undue hardship
options.D: A SARS assessment of the buyer's tax compliance status

questions.explanation: The NCA requires credit providers to conduct a thorough affordability assessment before granting credit.

questions.question: A buyer's bond application is declined due to an adverse credit listing. Under the NCA, what right does the buyer have?

options.A: The right to have the listing removed immediately by the credit bureau
options.B: No rights — adverse listings are final
options.C: The right to apply to the Property Practitioners Ombud for relief
options.D: The right to access their credit report free of charge once a year and to dispute inaccurate information with the credit bureau

questions.explanation: Under the NCA, every credit consumer has the right to access their credit report free of charge once per year.

questions.question: What is 'reckless lending' under the National Credit Act?

options.A: A bank charging interest above the prescribed maximum rate
options.B: A bank refusing to lend to first-time buyers
options.C: Granting credit to a consumer without conducting a proper affordability assessment, resulting in the consumer being over-indebted
options.D: A bank accepting insufficient security for a home loan

questions.explanation: The NCA prohibits reckless lending — granting credit without a proper assessment of the consumer's ability to repay.

questions.question: A buyer obtains a bond pre-approval certificate. What does this mean?

options.A: The bank has unconditionally approved the buyer's bond application
options.B: The bond has been registered at the Deeds Office
options.C: The bank has conditionally approved credit up to a specified amount, subject to property valuation and final verification
options.D: The buyer is guaranteed to receive the bond regardless of the property purchased

questions.explanation: A pre-approval certificate indicates the bank is willing to lend up to a certain amount, subject to conditions.

questions.question: What is a 'bond originator' and what service do they provide?

options.A: A legal professional who drafts bond registration documents
options.B: A bank employee who processes bond applications internally
options.C: An independent intermediary who submits a buyer's bond application to multiple banks simultaneously to secure the best interest rate and terms
options.D: A PPRA official who approves bond applications for first-time buyers

questions.explanation: A bond originator acts as an intermediary between buyers and multiple banks to secure competitive rates.

questions.question: A buyer's sale agreement is subject to bond approval within 21 days. On day 22, the buyer receives bond approval. What happens to the sale agreement?

options.A: The sale agreement automatically extends for another 7 days
options.B: The bond approval is still valid — the delay is inconsequential
options.C: The buyer may still accept the bond approval and proceed
options.D: The suspensive condition has not been fulfilled in time — the agreement may have lapsed, unless the seller agrees in writing to extend the deadline

questions.explanation: A suspensive condition with a deadline must be met within the specified period — missing it may cause the agreement to lapse.

questions.question: Under the NCA, what is the maximum interest rate a bank may charge on a home loan?

options.A: A flat rate of 12% per annum regardless of the repo rate
options.B: Prime plus 5%
options.C: The rate is subject to a prescribed maximum linked to the repo rate — the NCA sets a formula-based maximum that varies
options.D: There is no maximum — interest rates are freely negotiable

questions.explanation: The NCA prescribes a maximum interest rate formula tied to the repo rate.

questions.question: A bond originator earns commission from a bank for placing a home loan. Must this commission be disclosed to the buyer?

options.A: No — commission between the originator and bank is confidential
options.B: Only if the buyer specifically asks
options.C: Only if the commission exceeds R5 000
options.D: Yes — property practitioners including bond originators must disclose all commissions and fees received from third parties to their clients

questions.explanation: Disclosure is a core obligation under the Code of Conduct — all commissions received from third parties must be disclosed to clients.

questions.question: What is a 'bond registration attorney'?

options.A: An attorney who advises buyers on whether to take a bond
options.B: A legal professional appointed by the buyer to negotiate their bond
options.C: An attorney appointed by the bank to register the mortgage bond over the property in the Deeds Registry as security for the home loan
options.D: An attorney who prosecutes reckless lending cases under the NCA

questions.explanation: The bond registration attorney is appointed by and acts for the bank — their role is to register the bank's security interest.

questions.question: A buyer purchases a property and takes a bond. The bond is registered. What right does this give the bank?

options.A: The bank becomes a co-owner of the property
options.B: The bank may collect rental from the property directly
options.C: The bank may occupy the property during the loan term
options.D: The bank has a real right (mortgage) over the property — they may sell it in execution if the buyer defaults on the loan

questions.explanation: A registered mortgage bond gives the bank a real right of security over the property.

questions.question: A buyer wants to cancel their bond after registration. What costs are involved?

options.A: No costs — cancellation is free within 12 months
options.B: A fee equal to 3 months' interest
options.C: Bond cancellation attorney fees, and potentially early settlement penalties as per the credit agreement and the NCA
options.D: A standard government cancellation fee of R2 500

questions.explanation: Cancelling a registered bond involves bond cancellation attorney fees and potentially early settlement penalties.

questions.question: What is the role of the National Credit Regulator (NCR)?

options.A: To set the prime lending rate for all South African banks
options.B: To register and regulate property practitioners who originate bonds
options.C: To register and regulate credit providers, credit bureaux, and debt counsellors, and to enforce the National Credit Act
options.D: To manage the National Housing Fund for first-time buyers

questions.explanation: The NCR is the regulatory body established under the NCA to register and monitor compliance.

questions.question: A buyer applies for a bond but has a poor credit record. The bank grants the bond anyway at a very high interest rate without conducting an affordability assessment. This is an example of:

options.A: Aggressive but legal bank lending
options.B: A subprime mortgage — lawful under SA law
options.C: Standard risk-based pricing — no issues arise
options.D: Reckless lending in contravention of the NCA — the bank may face regulatory consequences and the agreement may be set aside

questions.explanation: The NCA prohibits granting credit without a proper affordability assessment — this is reckless lending.

questions.question: In a property transfer, who appoints the transferring attorney?

options.A: The buyer — as the person paying for the property
options.B: The bank — as the lender financing the purchase
options.C: The seller — it is a common law principle that the seller chooses the transferring attorney
options.D: The PPRA — to ensure compliance with the Act

questions.explanation: Under common law and well-established practice, the transferring attorney is appointed by the seller.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 'Deed of Transfer'?

options.A: It is the agreement between buyer and seller to transfer the property
options.B: It is the bond document registered in favour of the bank
options.C: It is the formal legal document registered at the Deeds Registry that passes ownership of immovable property from the seller to the buyer
options.D: It is the mandate agreement signed between the seller and estate agent

questions.explanation: The Deed of Transfer is the instrument of transfer registered in the Deeds Registry that passes ownership to the buyer.

questions.question: What is 'lodgement' in the conveyancing process?

options.A: The payment of transfer duty to SARS
options.B: The signing of the offer to purchase by both parties
options.C: The submission of all transfer documents to the Deeds Office by the conveyancer for examination and registration
options.D: The registration of the bond by the bank's attorney

questions.explanation: Lodgement is the formal step where the conveyancer submits the complete package of transfer documents to the Deeds Office.

questions.question: How long does the Deeds Office typically take to process and register a property transfer?

options.A: 24 hours
options.B: 7 days
options.C: Approximately 7–10 working days in most major Deeds Offices
options.D: 30 days

questions.explanation: The standard processing time at South African Deeds Offices is approximately 7–10 working days after lodgement.

questions.question: What is a 'Power of Attorney' in the context of a property transfer?

options.A: A document authorising the estate agent to sign offers on the buyer's behalf
options.B: A PPRA form authorising the practitioner to collect commission
options.C: A document authorising a specified person (usually the conveyancer) to sign transfer documents on behalf of the seller or buyer
options.D: A court order authorising the transfer of property without the seller's consent

questions.explanation: A Power of Attorney in property transfers authorises the transferring attorney to sign documents on behalf of the seller or buyer.

questions.question: What documents must a conveyancer obtain before lodging a transfer at the Deeds Office?

options.A: The offer to purchase only
options.B: The title deed and rates clearance certificate
options.C: Transfer duty receipt, rates clearance certificate, levy clearance certificate (if applicable), FICA compliance, and all required transfer documents
options.D: The estate agent's commission invoice and the buyer's bond approval letter

questions.explanation: Lodgement requires a complete package — SARS transfer duty receipt, municipal rates clearance, body corporate levy clearance, and full FICA compliance.

questions.question: A transfer is rejected by the Deeds Office due to an error in the Deed of Transfer. What happens?

options.A: The transaction is cancelled and a new sale agreement must be signed
options.B: The Deeds Office corrects the error and proceeds with registration
options.C: The documents are 'requisitioned' — returned to the conveyancer for correction and re-lodgement
options.D: The parties must appear in court to authorise the correction

questions.explanation: When the Deeds Office finds an error, it issues a 'requisition' — the documents are returned to the conveyancer for correction.

questions.question: At what point does the buyer become the legal owner of the property?

options.A: When the offer to purchase is signed by both parties
options.B: When the buyer pays the full purchase price
options.C: When the conveyancer confirms that all documents are in order
options.D: When the Deed of Transfer is formally registered at the Deeds Registry

questions.explanation: In South African law, ownership of immovable property passes only upon registration of the Deed of Transfer in the Deeds Registry.

questions.question: The transferring attorney receives the full purchase price in trust. When may they pay the seller?

options.A: Immediately upon receipt of the purchase price
options.B: 48 hours after receipt to allow for bank clearance
options.C: On the date the bond is approved
options.D: On the date of registration of transfer — the seller is only entitled to the proceeds once ownership has legally transferred to the buyer

questions.explanation: The seller is entitled to the purchase price on the date of registration — not before.

questions.question: What is a 'simultaneous transfer' (also called a 'chain' or 'linked' transfer)?

options.A: A transfer where buyer and seller sign simultaneously in the same office
options.B: A transfer processed simultaneously with a bond cancellation
options.C: Multiple related property transfers that are registered together in the Deeds Office on the same day
options.D: A transfer where two properties are swapped between two owners simultaneously

questions.explanation: Chain or linked transfers occur when multiple interdependent sales must register simultaneously.

questions.question: What is the difference between the transferring attorney and the bond registration attorney?

options.A: They are the same attorney — one person handles both
options.B: The transferring attorney acts for the bank; the bond attorney acts for the seller
options.C: The transferring attorney acts for the seller and passes ownership; the bond registration attorney acts for the bank and registers the mortgage
options.D: The transferring attorney is appointed by the PPRA; the bond attorney by the buyer

questions.explanation: The transferring attorney passes ownership; the bond registration attorney registers the bank's security — they perform different legal functions.

questions.question: A seller is deceased and the executor wants to sell property in the estate. What additional document does the conveyancer require?

options.A: A certified copy of the deceased's birth certificate
options.B: The deceased's last SARS tax return
options.C: The Letters of Executorship issued by the Master of the High Court
options.D: Consent from all heirs before the property may be advertised

questions.explanation: The executor's authority to deal with estate assets is derived from the Letters of Executorship.

questions.question: What is a 'Section 42 transfer' under the Administration of Estates Act?

options.A: A transfer to a creditor of the estate in settlement of a debt
options.B: A transfer of property from one living person to another
options.C: A transfer of property directly from a deceased estate to an heir or legatee in terms of the Liquidation and Distribution account
options.D: A transfer where the buyer takes over the seller's existing bond

questions.explanation: A section 42 transfer is a distribution of estate assets directly to a beneficiary in terms of the approved L&D account.

questions.question: A property practitioner refers a buyer to a specific conveyancing attorney in exchange for a referral fee. Is this permissible?

options.A: Yes — referral fees are standard practice in the property industry
options.B: Yes — provided the fee is disclosed to the buyer
options.C: No — receiving undisclosed inducements or kickbacks for referring clients to service providers is a breach of the Code of Conduct
options.D: Only permissible if the attorney also refers clients to the agent

questions.explanation: Accepting referral fees from conveyancers without full disclosure to the client is a conflict of interest.

questions.question: What does the Deeds Registry do at registration of transfer?

options.A: It values the property and confirms the purchase price is fair
options.B: It confirms that the seller is financially solvent
options.C: It ensures the buyer has sufficient funds to pay the purchase price
options.D: It examines the transfer documents for legal correctness and registers the change of ownership in the official register

questions.explanation: The Deeds Registry performs a legal examination of transfer documents — not a financial audit.

questions.question: What does 'pro rata' mean in a property context?

options.A: A Latin term meaning 'as agreed by the parties'
options.B: The process of dividing transfer duty between buyer and seller
options.C: In proportion — used to describe how costs such as rates and levies are apportioned between buyer and seller based on the transfer date
options.D: A valuation method used by municipalities

questions.explanation: Pro rata apportionment divides costs between buyer and seller based on the portion of the month or year each party owned the property.

questions.question: What is 'usufruct' in South African property law?

options.A: The right to sell a property on behalf of the owner
options.B: A registered restriction preventing a property from being bonded
options.C: A personal servitude giving a person the right to use and enjoy another's property and its fruits for their lifetime
options.D: The right of a tenant to renew their lease indefinitely

questions.explanation: A usufruct is a personal servitude allowing the usufructuary to use and enjoy the property and its income for their lifetime.

questions.question: What is 'prescription' in the context of South African property law?

options.A: A doctor's recommendation for a building inspection
options.B: A legal requirement to register all property transfers within 12 months
options.C: The extinction of a legal right or debt through the passage of time without enforcement
options.D: A PPRA regulation requiring annual FFC renewal

questions.explanation: Prescription refers to the loss of a legal claim due to the passage of time — most debts prescribe after 3 years.

questions.question: What does 'res perit domino' mean in a property sale?

options.A: The seller's right to retain the property after receiving payment
options.B: The buyer's right to inspect before transfer
options.C: 'The thing perishes for the owner' — the risk of damage or destruction passes to the buyer once the sale is concluded, even before transfer
options.D: The estate agent's right to commission upon effective cause

questions.explanation: Once a valid sale agreement is signed, the risk of accidental damage passes to the buyer — buyers should insure the property from the date of the sale agreement.

questions.question: What is 'unjust enrichment' in the context of property transactions?

options.A: A buyer paying more than market value for a property
options.B: An estate agent earning excess commission
options.C: A legal principle requiring a person who has been enriched at another's expense without a legal basis to return that enrichment
options.D: A seller receiving more than the asking price at auction

questions.explanation: Unjust enrichment arises when one party benefits at another's expense without legal justification.

questions.question: What is a 'fideicommissum' in South African property law?

options.A: A mortgage bond registered in favour of a bank
options.B: A restriction preventing a property from being sold for a fixed period
options.C: A testamentary or contractual arrangement where property is left to one person on condition that it passes to another on a certain event
options.D: A penalty clause in a lease agreement

questions.explanation: A fideicommissum is a common estate planning tool — the fiduciary holds the property for their lifetime then it passes to the fideicommissaries.

questions.question: What is 'expropriation' in the context of South African property law?

options.A: The voluntary sale of property to the government at market value
options.B: The cancellation of a title deed by the Deeds Registry for non-payment of rates
options.C: The compulsory acquisition of private property by the state for a public purpose, subject to compensation
options.D: The forced transfer of property between family members by court order

questions.explanation: Expropriation is the compulsory acquisition of property by the government for public purposes — just and equitable compensation must be paid.

questions.question: What does 'pactum de non cedendo' mean?

options.A: An agreement that no commission will be charged on a sale
options.B: A clause preventing tenants from subletting without consent
options.C: A contractual clause preventing a party from ceding (transferring) their rights under the agreement to a third party
options.D: An agreement not to sell a property for a specified period

questions.explanation: A pactum de non cedendo is a prohibition on cession — preventing the transfer of contractual rights to someone else.

questions.question: What is a 'warranty of title' in a property sale?

options.A: A guarantee by the estate agent that the property is defect-free
options.B: A PPRA certification that the property has been properly valued
options.C: A guarantee by the seller that they have valid, unencumbered title to the property and the right to sell it
options.D: A bank's guarantee that the buyer's bond will be approved

questions.explanation: A warranty of title is the seller's guarantee that they are the lawful owner and have the right to sell the property free of undisclosed encumbrances.

questions.question: What is meant by 'time is of the essence' in a property sale agreement?

options.A: That the sale must be completed within 30 days by law
options.B: That the parties must act quickly to avoid market changes
options.C: The specified deadlines in the agreement are strictly binding — failure to meet them may entitle the innocent party to cancel the agreement
options.D: A valuation term meaning the market timing is optimal for the seller

questions.explanation: When a contract states 'time is of the essence,' compliance with deadlines is a material term — missing them may entitle the other party to cancel.

questions.question: Agent Naledi holds a sole mandate. During it, the seller's brother-in-law (not a registered agent) introduces a buyer who concludes a valid sale. The seller argues no commission is owed. What is the correct legal position?

options.A: No commission is owed — the buyer was introduced by a private individual
options.B: Half commission is owed — shared between Naledi and the brother-in-law
options.C: The commission must be paid to the brother-in-law as he was the effective cause
options.D: Naledi is entitled to full commission — the sole mandate was active and the seller may not circumvent it, regardless of who introduced the buyer

questions.explanation: A sole mandate protects the agent from exactly this scenario — during the mandate period, any sale entitles the sole mandate holder to commission.

questions.question: A buyer makes an offer before a sale agreement is signed. Before the seller signs, the buyer phones to withdraw the offer. The seller subsequently signs and returns the accepted offer. Is there a binding agreement?

options.A: No — the buyer withdrew before the seller accepted
options.B: Yes — the seller accepted the formal written offer
options.C: Yes — the withdrawal must be in writing to be effective
options.D: Only if the withdrawal was communicated to the seller before acceptance

questions.explanation: An offer may be revoked before acceptance, but the revocation must be communicated to the offeree before acceptance for it to be effective.

questions.question: An estate agent presents an offer to the seller who verbally accepts. Before the offer is signed by the seller, the buyer withdraws the offer. The agent claims commission because the seller verbally accepted. Is the agent entitled to commission?

options.A: Yes — verbal acceptance by the seller creates a binding contract
options.B: Yes — the agent was the effective cause of a near-concluded sale
options.C: Yes — provided the agent can prove the verbal acceptance occurred
options.D: No — a valid sale agreement for immovable property must be in writing and signed; a verbal acceptance creates no enforceable contract

questions.explanation: The Alienation of Land Act requires a sale of immovable property to be in writing and signed — verbal acceptance creates no binding contract.

questions.question: Agent Precious concludes a sale at R3 500 000 with 5% commission in the mandate. Before transfer, buyer and seller agree to reduce the price to R3 200 000 without consulting Precious. What commission is Precious entitled to?

options.A: 5% of R3 200 000 — the final agreed price
options.B: 2.5% of R3 500 000 — a compromise
options.C: No commission — the parties renegotiated without her
options.D: 5% of R3 500 000 — the original mandated price — unless Precious agreed in writing to the reduction

questions.explanation: The mandate fixes the commission — if the price was reduced without the agent's consent, the agent may still be entitled to commission on the original mandated price.

questions.question: A property practitioner receives a R500 000 deposit from a buyer on Monday. On Wednesday, the seller calls demanding the deposit be released immediately as they need cash urgently. What must the practitioner do?

options.A: Release the deposit — the seller is the client and their instruction must be followed
options.B: Release half the deposit as a compromise
options.C: Release the deposit if the sale agreement permits early release
options.D: Decline to release the deposit without a lawful instruction that complies with the Act — pre-registration release of sale proceeds is prohibited

questions.explanation: The Property Practitioners Act prohibits the release of sale proceeds before registration of transfer.
