questions.question: A principal discovers their agency has been using trust funds to pay staff salaries for the past 3 months. What has occurred and what should happen?

options.A: A temporary cash flow solution — common in small agencies
options.B: An administrative error that must be corrected at year-end audit
options.C: A breach only if the funds are not replaced within 30 days
options.D: A serious criminal offence — the funds must be replaced immediately and the matter reported to the PPRA

questions.explanation: Borrowing from trust — even temporarily with intention to repay — constitutes misappropriation of client funds. This is a criminal offence.

questions.question: A practitioner receives two identical offers on a property. One is from a buyer they know personally who needs more time to arrange their finances. The other is from an unknown buyer who is ready immediately. What must the practitioner do?

options.A: Choose the known buyer — loyalty is a professional value
options.B: Present both offers and advise the seller to take the ready buyer
options.C: Delay the unknown buyer's offer to give the known buyer more time
options.D: Present both offers honestly to the seller with all relevant information — choosing favourites or manipulating the timing of offers to benefit a friend is a conflict of interest

questions.explanation: Personal relationships cannot influence how the practitioner handles offers — both must be presented simultaneously with honest, unbiased information.

questions.question: A buyer's offer includes a 'subject to bond approval' condition. The bank approves a bond but at an interest rate significantly higher than the buyer expected. The buyer refuses to proceed. Is the suspensive condition fulfilled?

options.A: No — the buyer expected a lower rate and is entitled to withdraw
options.B: No — the bond approval must match the terms the buyer intended
options.C: It depends on whether the offer to purchase specified an interest rate
options.D: Yes — unless the offer to purchase specifies the maximum interest rate the buyer will accept, any bond approval regardless of rate fulfils the suspensive condition

questions.explanation: A bond approval condition is fulfilled when the bank approves any bond, unless the sale agreement specifies acceptable terms such as a maximum interest rate.

questions.question: A buyer signs an offer to purchase subject to 'the seller vacating the property by the date of transfer.' The seller is unable to vacate on time due to a family medical emergency. The buyer wants to cancel immediately. What is the position?

options.A: The buyer may cancel immediately — the condition was not met
options.B: The seller's medical emergency automatically extends the period
options.C: Force majeure applies and neither party bears responsibility
options.D: The legal position depends on whether the clause was a suspensive condition or a warranty — legal advice is needed before cancellation

questions.explanation: Whether a seller's inability to vacate by transfer date allows immediate cancellation depends on how the clause was drafted and the specific circumstances.

questions.question: Under the Financial Intelligence Centre Act (FICA), property practitioners are classified as:

options.A: Exempt institutions — FICA only applies to banks
options.B: Reporting institutions with limited obligations
options.C: Accountable institutions with full FICA obligations including KYC and suspicious transaction reporting
options.D: Supervised institutions under the jurisdiction of the JSE

questions.explanation: Property practitioners are listed as accountable institutions under FICA with full obligations including KYC and suspicious transaction reporting.

questions.question: A property practitioner is approached by a client who wants to purchase a property using funds they claim were inherited from a foreign relative. The client is reluctant to provide documentation. What FICA obligation applies?

options.A: Accept the client's verbal explanation — FICA only applies to South African funds
options.B: Proceed with the transaction — the funds are in a South African bank account
options.C: Ask for a brief written explanation from the client and proceed
options.D: Conduct full FICA due diligence — verify the source of funds with documentation; if the client refuses, decline the transaction and consider whether a Suspicious Transaction Report is required

questions.explanation: FICA requires property practitioners to establish the source of funds for property transactions — reluctance to provide documentation is a red flag for potential money laundering.

questions.question: A property practitioner is selling a property on behalf of a company director who later reveals the company is insolvent and under liquidation. The sale was concluded but not yet transferred. What must the practitioner do?

options.A: Proceed with transfer — the sale is concluded
options.B: Cancel the transaction immediately
options.C: Proceed if the director personally guarantees the proceeds
options.D: Halt the process and seek urgent legal advice — a director of an insolvent company may lack authority to sell company assets once liquidation commences

questions.explanation: Company insolvency vests control of assets in the liquidator, removing the directors' authority — the practitioner must obtain legal advice before proceeding.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked by a seller to keep the sale price strictly confidential. After the sale, a neighbour asks what the property sold for. What should the practitioner do?

options.A: Share the price — it becomes public record after registration
options.B: Share the price — there is no legal basis for confidentiality after a sale
options.C: Share only with other registered practitioners who have a professional need
options.D: Decline to share the information — the seller's instruction must be respected, and the Deeds Registry is the appropriate public source

questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct requires agents to respect client confidentiality — even though sale prices become public record at the Deeds Registry.

questions.question: A property practitioner receives a complaint that they never responded to post-sale queries about the transfer process. The agent argues they have no obligation to assist buyers post-sale. What is the correct position?

options.A: Correct — the agent's obligation ends when the sale is concluded
options.B: Correct — post-sale queries should go to the conveyancer
options.C: Partially correct — only major queries require a response
options.D: Incorrect — a professional property practitioner is expected to maintain communication and assist clients throughout the transaction including post-sale

questions.explanation: Professional service does not end at signing — the Code of Conduct requires practitioners to act in clients' best interests throughout the transaction.

questions.question: A property practitioner's social media post about a property sale includes a photo of the seller and buyer without their consent. What legislation is primarily relevant?

options.A: The Consumer Protection Act — it regulates advertising
options.B: The PPRA Code of Conduct only
options.C: The Copyright Act — the photo belongs to the photographer
options.D: The Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) — sharing identifiable personal information without consent is a POPIA violation, in addition to being a Code of Conduct breach

questions.explanation: POPIA regulates the processing of personal information — publishing a photo that identifies individuals without their consent is a POPIA breach.

questions.question: A foreign national wants to purchase property in South Africa. May foreign nationals own property in SA?

options.A: Foreign nationals may not own property in South Africa
options.B: Foreign nationals may only own property through a South African company
options.C: Foreign nationals may own property only in coastal areas
options.D: Foreign nationals may generally own immovable property in South Africa with no restriction — however, specific FICA due diligence and exchange control considerations apply

questions.explanation: South Africa does not prohibit foreign nationals from owning immovable property — but FICA due diligence and exchange control regulations apply.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to facilitate a sale where the buyer and seller agree to report a lower purchase price to SARS than was actually paid. What must the practitioner do?

options.A: Facilitate the transaction — the parties are both adults who agreed
options.B: Report only the price both parties agreed on
options.C: Refuse to be named on the sale agreement but facilitate the transaction
options.D: Refuse absolutely — knowingly participating in a transaction that misreports the purchase price to SARS is tax evasion, making the practitioner complicit in a criminal offence

questions.explanation: Misreporting sale prices to SARS to reduce transfer duty or CGT is tax evasion — a criminal offence. No client instruction can justify this.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to assess whether a property qualifies as a 'primary residence' for CGT purposes. What determines primary residence status?

options.A: The property where the seller's car is registered
options.B: The property nearest to the seller's workplace
options.C: The property with the highest market value
options.D: The property that the owner primarily uses as their ordinary residence — SARS looks at where the owner sleeps most nights, where their family lives, and overall lifestyle indicators

questions.explanation: SARS determines primary residence status based on factual analysis of where the owner primarily lives — a person may only have one primary residence at a time for CGT purposes.

questions.question: A property practitioner is marketing a property that has an existing offer from the seller's brother at below market value. The agent has a higher offer from an independent buyer. The seller insists on accepting the related party offer. What must the agent do?

options.A: Accept the seller's decision — it is their property
options.B: Report the seller to the PPRA for potential fraud
options.C: Refuse the mandate entirely
options.D: Present both offers to the seller with independent professional advice on the value difference, document their advice in writing, and ultimately respect the seller's decision — while considering their FICA obligations if the transaction appears to evade taxes

questions.explanation: The agent must provide documented professional advice about the value difference and ultimately respect the seller's decision — but consider FICA implications if tax evasion is suspected.

questions.question: A property practitioner discovers their agency's principal has been accepting bribes from a conveyancing firm to refer all transfer work to them, regardless of quality or cost to clients. What must the practitioner do?

options.A: Stay silent — the principal's business decisions are their own
options.B: Report it to the conveyancing firm's Law Society representative only
options.C: Only act if a client specifically complains
options.D: Report the conduct to the PPRA and consider reporting to the relevant legal professional body — accepting bribes to refer work creates a conflict of interest that harms clients

questions.explanation: Accepting bribes (kickbacks) from service providers in exchange for referrals is corruption — both the PPRA and the Legal Practice Council have jurisdiction.

questions.question: A practitioner is asked by a developer to market a property in an area they know is zoned for residential use. The developer presents approved building plans for a mixed-use development including retail space. After sales are concluded, buyers discover the retail zoning approval was refused. What is the agent's position?

options.A: The agent is not liable — they relied on the developer's representations
options.B: The agent is liable only if they personally made the zoning claim
options.C: The agent bears no liability — approved building plans were presented
options.D: The agent's position depends on whether they verified the zoning — having approved building plans is not the same as approved rezoning. A professional practitioner should verify that zoning supports the advertised use.

questions.explanation: Building plans can be 'approved' for existing zoning without a rezoning application being decided — a practitioner marketing a mixed-use development must verify the zoning supports the mixed use.

questions.question: An agent's social media profile describes them as a 'PPRA-certified master property expert.' There is no such designation. What has the practitioner done?

options.A: Used acceptable marketing language that is common in the industry
options.B: Made a minor misrepresentation with no practical consequence
options.C: Used a title that the PPRA will automatically approve on request
options.D: Created a false professional designation — misrepresenting qualifications or regulatory status is a Code of Conduct breach and potentially a contravention of the Consumer Protection Act

questions.explanation: Property practitioners may only claim qualifications and designations they actually hold — inventing a non-existent designation is clear misrepresentation.

questions.question: The PPRA fines a property practitioner R50 000 for a Code of Conduct breach. The practitioner disagrees with the finding. What is their remedy?

options.A: Apply to the High Court immediately for a review
options.B: Pay under protest and accept the outcome
options.C: Request the PPRA to reconsider the fine
options.D: The practitioner may appeal the decision through the PPRA's prescribed appeal process — the Act provides for internal appeals before external remedies such as High Court review

questions.explanation: The Act provides for an internal appeal process against PPRA disciplinary decisions — internal remedies should be exhausted before approaching the courts.

questions.question: A property is subject to a High Court interdict preventing any alienation. The property practitioner is marketing the property. What must they do?

options.A: Continue marketing — interdicts do not affect marketing
options.B: Inform the seller and continue — the interdict is the seller's problem
options.C: Immediately cancel the mandate and return all marketing fees
options.D: Halt all marketing immediately and advise the seller to seek legal advice — an interdict preventing alienation legally prevents the property from being sold

questions.explanation: A High Court interdict preventing alienation is a court order that must be complied with — marketing a property that legally cannot be sold while knowing of the interdict could constitute contempt of court.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked by a client to negotiate an 'all-in' price with a developer that includes transfer costs. What does this mean and what issues arise?

options.A: The total price includes commission — no other costs apply
options.B: The price includes VAT only
options.C: The price covers the property plus all taxes and fees with no additional costs to the buyer
options.D: An 'all-in' price typically means the developer covers transfer costs as a selling incentive — however, the practitioner must ensure clients understand exactly what is included as 'all-in' is not a legally defined term

questions.explanation: 'All-in' pricing is a common developer incentive but lacks a standardised definition — the practitioner must ensure the sale agreement clearly specifies exactly which costs are covered.

questions.question: A property practitioner is facilitating a sale where the buyer intends to pay R1 million in cash and R2 million via bank transfer. What FICA obligations arise regarding the cash portion?

options.A: Standard FICA verification only — no additional cash-specific obligations
options.B: A Cash Threshold Report for R1 million and an STR for the overall transaction
options.C: Report the full R3 million transaction to the FIC
options.D: A Cash Threshold Report must be submitted to the FIC for the R1 million cash portion — additionally, a cash transaction of this size in a property deal is unusual and likely requires an STR. Enhanced due diligence on the source of cash funds is mandatory.

questions.explanation: Any cash transaction of R50 000 or more requires a CTR — R1 million in cash for a property purchase is extremely unusual and represents a significant money laundering risk.

questions.question: A property practitioner receives two simultaneous offers on a sole mandate property: one from a buyer they introduced themselves, and one from a sub-agent. Both offers are for the same amount. The principal instructs the agent to accept their own buyer's offer to avoid paying the sub-agent. Is this permissible?

options.A: Yes — the agent holding the sole mandate may choose which offer to present
options.B: Yes — it is logical to avoid paying additional commission
options.C: No — both offers must be rejected and the property remarketed
options.D: The agent must present both offers honestly to the seller and advise them impartially — allowing personal commission gain to influence which offer is recommended is a conflict of interest

questions.explanation: An agent has a fiduciary duty to act in the seller's best interest — steering the seller toward an offer that maximises the agent's own commission is a conflict of interest.

questions.question: A property practitioner markets a property described as 'waterfront' when it is approximately 500 metres from the nearest water feature. What has occurred?

options.A: A minor inaccuracy — 'waterfront' is a marketing term only
options.B: An acceptable exaggeration in property marketing
options.C: A breach only if a buyer purchased based solely on this description
options.D: False advertising — 'waterfront' has a specific meaning to buyers and using it misleadingly constitutes misrepresentation under the Code of Conduct and potentially the Consumer Protection Act

questions.explanation: Specific property descriptors like 'waterfront' carry precise meanings for buyers — using them inaccurately is misrepresentation.

questions.question: A property practitioner has been practising for 20 years but has never used a CRM system and keeps all client records on paper. A fire destroys all records. What is the minimum the practitioner must have retained and for how long?

options.A: 1 year of records — anything older is unnecessary
options.B: 3 years of records in any format
options.C: 7 years of financial records only
options.D: 5 years of business and trust account records as required by the Property Practitioners Act — the medium is not prescribed but the 5-year period is mandatory. The loss in a fire is a mitigating factor but does not remove the obligation.

questions.explanation: The Act requires 5 years of records regardless of the storage medium — best practice is electronic backup precisely to prevent fire loss.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to give evidence in a civil dispute between a buyer and seller about a transaction they facilitated. The practitioner's file notes clearly support the buyer's version. What must the practitioner do?

options.A: Decline to testify — it may damage the seller, who was their client
options.B: Testify only for the party who pays their appearance fees
options.C: Provide only factual information without expressing any opinion
options.D: Provide honest, complete evidence based on their records — a practitioner cannot withhold or distort evidence to protect a client

questions.explanation: A practitioner called as a witness is obliged to provide honest evidence — withholding relevant records or distorting testimony to protect a client could constitute contempt of court or perjury.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to conduct a Comparative Market Analysis (CMA). What is the primary methodology used?

options.A: Calculating the replacement cost of the building only
options.B: Applying a standard percentage to the municipal valuation
options.C: Averaging the asking prices of properties currently listed nearby
options.D: Analysing recent confirmed sale prices of comparable properties in the same area, adjusted for differences in size, condition, and features

questions.explanation: A CMA uses the sales comparison approach — analysing recent actual sales (not asking prices) of comparable properties.

questions.question: A property is sold 'with reserve' and 'voetstoots' at auction. A buyer bids R2 800 000 and wins. After the auction, the buyer discovers the property has significant structural damage not mentioned in the auction catalogue. What remedy does the buyer have?

options.A: No remedy — voetstoots applies to auction sales
options.B: Full refund — the auction catalogue created a warranty
options.C: Remedy only if the auctioneer made specific representations
options.D: If the seller knew of the structural damage and deliberately concealed it, the voetstoots clause does not protect them — deliberate concealment of known latent defects constitutes fraud regardless of the voetstoots clause

questions.explanation: The voetstoots clause does not protect against deliberate concealment of known defects even at auction.

questions.question: What legislation primarily governs sectional title schemes?

options.A: The Property Practitioners Act 22 of 2019
options.B: The Deeds Registries Act 47 of 1937
options.C: The Sectional Titles Act 95 of 1986 and the Sectional Titles Schemes Management Act 8 of 2011
options.D: The Housing Act 107 of 1997

questions.explanation: Sectional title schemes are governed by the Sectional Titles Act and the Sectional Titles Schemes Management Act.

questions.question: A retirement village unit is sold under a 'life right' scheme. What does the buyer actually acquire?

options.A: Full ownership of the unit in their own name
options.B: A sectional title interest in the development
options.C: A share block in the retirement company
options.D: The right to occupy the unit for the remainder of their life — a life right is not ownership. The developer retains ownership of the property.

questions.explanation: A life right is a personal right of occupation — not ownership. The buyer cannot sell, bond, or bequeath the unit.

questions.question: What is 'negative equity' in the context of a home loan?

options.A: When a property generates negative rental yield
options.B: When the buyer pays more than market value
options.C: When the bank earns less interest than expected
options.D: When the outstanding bond balance exceeds the current market value of the property — the owner would receive less from a sale than they owe the bank

questions.explanation: Negative equity occurs when market values fall below the outstanding bond — this traps owners who cannot sell without paying the shortfall.

questions.question: A property practitioner markets a property. An offer is made and accepted by the seller. Before transfer, the seller discovers the property is subject to a prior agreement of sale (pre-emptive right) in favour of a neighbour registered 10 years ago. What must happen?

options.A: The current sale proceeds — a 10-year-old right has prescribed
options.B: The neighbour must be notified and given a chance to match the offer
options.C: The current sale may proceed if the neighbour doesn't respond within 7 days
options.D: The registered pre-emptive right must be addressed before transfer — even if the right may have prescribed, this cannot be assumed. Legal advice is required.

questions.explanation: A registered pre-emptive right is a real right that must be formally cancelled before transfer — whether it has prescribed requires legal analysis.

questions.question: Interest rates rise by 2% nationally. What is the most likely immediate impact on the residential property market?

options.A: Property prices rise as sellers demand more
options.B: No impact — property prices are set by comparable sales, not interest rates
options.C: Instant collapse of all property values
options.D: Reduced buyer affordability typically leading to lower demand, longer selling times, and downward pressure on prices

questions.explanation: Interest rate increases directly affect affordability — higher bond repayments reduce the pool of qualifying buyers and create downward pressure on prices.

questions.question: A property practitioner has been practising for 5 years and wants to apply for accreditation as a PDE examiner with the PPRA. What is the primary qualification requirement?

options.A: 5 years of practice and a valid FFC are sufficient
options.B: Any property practitioner with an FFC may apply
options.C: Only PPRA board members may be PDE examiners
options.D: Principal status (PDE5 and NQF Level 5) is the minimum requirement — the PPRA appoints experienced qualified principals as PDE examiners

questions.explanation: The PPRA selects PDE examiners from qualified, experienced principals — principal status (PDE5, NQF Level 5) is required.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to verify whether a property is free of servitudes before accepting a mandate. Where can they obtain authoritative information?

options.A: From the seller's declaration only — no official verification is necessary
options.B: From the municipality's rates department
options.C: From the PPRA's property register
options.D: From the Deeds Registry — a title deed search reveals all registered servitudes, bonds, and endorsements against the property

questions.explanation: The Deeds Registry maintains a comprehensive record of all registered rights and encumbrances over immovable property.

questions.question: A property practitioner receives a mandate to sell a property at R5 million. The seller is also considering a rental option at R40 000 per month. The seller asks which option is better financially (ignoring tax). What should the agent advise?

options.A: The sale — immediate capital is always better
options.B: The rental — R40 000 per month provides better long-term return
options.C: The agent should not advise on financial decisions — refer to a financial advisor
options.D: The agent should present the financial comparison factually and recommend the seller take financial and tax advice for a comprehensive analysis

questions.explanation: A property practitioner may present factual financial comparisons but should not make definitive financial recommendations — this is the domain of a financial advisor.

questions.question: A property practitioner receives a notice of intention to withdraw their FFC from the PPRA. They believe the notice was issued in error. What is the correct procedural response?

options.A: Ignore the notice and continue trading — the burden of proof is on the PPRA
options.B: Apply directly to the High Court for an urgent interdict
options.C: Stop trading immediately and accept the withdrawal
options.D: Respond formally within the prescribed period stated in the notice, providing all relevant documentation and arguments against the withdrawal — the Act provides a right of response before withdrawal is finalised

questions.explanation: The Act provides procedural fairness — a practitioner must be given an opportunity to respond before their FFC is withdrawn. Ignoring the notice may result in automatic withdrawal.

questions.question: A PPRA inspector visits an agency unannounced and requests all trust account records for the past 5 years. The agency's bookkeeper says the inspector must make an appointment. What is the correct position?

options.A: The manager is correct — inspectors must give 48 hours' notice
options.B: The manager is correct — only auditors may inspect records
options.C: Inspectors may only inspect with a court order
options.D: The PPRA has the right to conduct unannounced inspections — the practitioner must cooperate; obstructing a PPRA inspector is a serious offence

questions.explanation: The PPRA has statutory powers of inspection including the right to conduct unannounced inspections — refusing to cooperate is a contravention of the Act.

questions.question: A property practitioner's FFC is withdrawn by the PPRA. The practitioner continues conducting transactions for a further month, believing their appeal suspends the withdrawal. What is the correct position?

options.A: An appeal automatically suspends the FFC withdrawal
options.B: The practitioner may continue for 30 days pending appeal
options.C: The practitioner may continue only for concluded transactions
options.D: An appeal does not automatically suspend the withdrawal unless the PPRA or a court specifically grants a suspension — the practitioner must apply for a suspension of the withdrawal pending appeal

questions.explanation: Filing an appeal does not automatically stay the effect of a PPRA decision — the practitioner must apply specifically for a suspension order.

questions.question: A property practitioner is conviced of driving under the influence of alcohol (DUI). The conviction is unrelated to their property practice. Must they disclose this to the PPRA?

options.A: No — a DUI is unrelated to property practice and need not be disclosed
options.B: Only if the PPRA specifically asks about criminal convictions
options.C: Only if the conviction resulted in imprisonment
options.D: Yes — the Act requires disclosure of criminal convictions. Non-disclosure of a conviction — even for matters unrelated to property practice — can itself be a ground for disciplinary action

questions.explanation: The Property Practitioners Act requires disclosure of criminal convictions — non-disclosure is itself a breach of the Act.

questions.question: A property practitioner is found guilty of a Code of Conduct breach. The PPRA imposes a fine of R50 000 and a 6-month FFC suspension. The fine is paid. During the suspension, the practitioner's spouse continues operating the same agency. What is the regulatory position?

options.A: The suspension only applies to the guilty practitioner — the spouse is unaffected
options.B: The agency may continue operating under the spouse's FFC
options.C: The agency must close for the 6-month suspension period
options.D: The suspended practitioner may not conduct business during the suspension. If the spouse is independently registered, they may continue operating — however, any informal involvement by the suspended practitioner would violate the suspension

questions.explanation: A suspension is personal to the registered practitioner — however, any informal involvement by the suspended practitioner in the agency's operations during the suspension may constitute a breach.

questions.question: Agent Naledi holds a sole mandate on a property from 1 February to 31 March. On 10 March, the seller's brother-in-law (not a registered agent) introduces a buyer who concludes a valid sale. The seller argues no commission is owed because the brother-in-law found the buyer. What is the correct legal position?

options.A: No commission is owed — the buyer was introduced by a private individual
options.B: Half commission is owed — shared between Naledi and the brother-in-law
options.C: The commission must be paid to the brother-in-law as he was the effective cause
options.D: Naledi is entitled to full commission — the sole mandate was active and the seller may not circumvent it, regardless of who introduced the buyer

questions.explanation: A sole mandate protects the agent from exactly this scenario — during the mandate period, any sale entitles the sole mandate holder to commission regardless of who introduced the buyer.

questions.question: A buyer makes an offer to purchase 'subject to selling my current property within 30 days.' This is an example of:

options.A: A resolutive condition
options.B: A warranty
options.C: A voetstoots clause
options.D: A suspensive condition — the offer only becomes binding if and when the buyer's property is sold within the 30-day period

questions.explanation: A 'subject to sale of buyer's property' clause is a classic suspensive condition — the agreement is not binding until the condition is fulfilled.

questions.question: A buyer's offer includes a '72-hour clause.' What does this mean?

options.A: The buyer has 72 hours to withdraw the offer
options.B: The seller must accept the offer within 72 hours
options.C: The offer expires automatically after 72 hours
options.D: The seller may continue marketing and if they receive another acceptable offer, they must give the original buyer 72 hours to fulfil any outstanding suspensive conditions or the seller may accept the new offer

questions.explanation: A 72-hour clause protects the seller against being locked into a suspended offer indefinitely — if a better offer arrives, the original buyer has 72 hours to waive their conditions or lose the deal.

questions.question: A property practitioner receives a complaint. The PPRA's investigation finds insufficient evidence to proceed. The complainant is unhappy with this outcome. What options do they have?

options.A: No further options — the PPRA's decision is final
options.B: Re-submit the same complaint with additional witnesses
options.C: Apply directly to the Master of the High Court for intervention
options.D: The complainant may request reasons for the decision under PAJA, consider whether to approach the courts for judicial review, or pursue a civil claim against the practitioner independently of the PPRA process

questions.explanation: A PPRA finding of insufficient evidence does not prevent a civil claim — the complainant may also review whether the PPRA followed proper procedures.

questions.question: A property practitioner discovers mid-transaction that the transfer attorney has been appointed by the seller but is also the seller's personal attorney who handles all their legal affairs. Should the practitioner advise the buyer?

options.A: No — the seller's choice of attorney is not the agent's concern
options.B: No — conveyancers always act impartially regardless of prior relationships
options.C: Yes — but only if the buyer asks about the attorney's independence
options.D: Yes — the buyer should be informed that the transferring attorney has an existing relationship with the seller, so the buyer can make an informed decision about whether to appoint their own attorney

questions.explanation: Informing the buyer allows them to make an informed decision about whether to appoint their own attorney to independently review documents.

questions.question: A property practitioner's sole mandate requires them to find a buyer 'ready, willing, and able to purchase.' A buyer who meets these criteria makes an offer that the seller irrationally rejects. Does the agent earn commission?

options.A: No — commission is only earned on a concluded sale
options.B: No — the seller's rejection of the offer means there was no sale
options.C: Yes — the agent produced a buyer but the seller frustrated the sale
options.D: Potentially yes — if the agent fulfilled their mandate obligation by producing a compliant buyer and the seller irrationally rejected the offer, the agent may have a commission claim for frustrating the sale

questions.explanation: Some mandates specify commission is earned when a suitable buyer is produced — if the mandate uses this language and the seller irrationally refuses, the agent may argue the commission was earned.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to facilitate the transfer of a property from a natural person to their own wholly-owned company. What taxes apply?

options.A: No taxes — the same person is the beneficial owner throughout
options.B: Transfer duty only at the reduced 'family' rate
options.C: Transfer duty at the standard rate on market value plus CGT for the seller
options.D: Transfer duty at the standard rate on the market value of the property and potentially CGT for the natural person on any gain — the same beneficial owner does not exempt the transaction from tax

questions.explanation: The law looks at the legal parties to a transaction, not the beneficial owner — a transfer from a natural person to their company is a transfer between two different legal entities and both transfer duty and potentially CGT apply.

questions.question: A buyer signs an offer to purchase. Three days later, the buyer's financial advisor tells them the property is overpriced and they should not have signed. The buyer wants to cancel. What are their options?

options.A: Cancel freely within 7 days — all property sales have a cooling-off period
options.B: Cancel by paying a 5% penalty
options.C: Cancel only if they can prove the property is overpriced
options.D: The buyer has very limited options — there is no general cooling-off period for property sales above a certain value. The buyer is bound by the signed agreement and would be in breach if they cancel without justification.

questions.explanation: Unlike some consumer contracts, private property sales above a certain value do not have a statutory cooling-off period — a buyer who signs an offer to purchase is bound by it.

questions.question: A buyer makes a purchase offer specifying 'transfer to be effected within 60 days.' Due to a dispute between the seller and their previous conveyancer about unpaid legal fees, the transfer is delayed to day 75. The buyer threatens to cancel. What is the correct analysis?

options.A: The buyer may cancel immediately — the 60-day deadline was not met
options.B: The seller's dispute with the conveyancer is force majeure — no breach
options.C: Transfer timelines are never binding unless 'time is of the essence' is stated
options.D: The analysis depends on whether the 60-day provision was a condition or an estimate, and whether 'time is of the essence' was stated — without this, the buyer typically must give notice of breach and a reasonable further period before cancelling

questions.explanation: Transfer timelines are not always strictly binding conditions — without a 'time is of the essence' clause, a delay may give rise to a breach claim but not automatic cancellation.

questions.question: A property practitioner facilitates a sale of a property. After registration, the buyer discovers the property is affected by a praedial servitude in favour of a neighbouring farm that allows vehicles to cross the property daily. This was not in the title deed provided by the seller. What has likely occurred?

options.A: The servitude is invalid — it was not in the title deed
options.B: The buyer must accept the servitude — it was registered somewhere
options.C: The servitude is a patent defect that the buyer should have seen
options.D: The title deed provided may have been incomplete — praedial servitudes may be registered in the neighbouring property's title deed rather than only in the servient property's deed. The practitioner should have conducted a comprehensive deeds search including neighbouring title deeds.

questions.explanation: Praedial servitudes may be registered on either the dominant or servient tenement — a comprehensive deeds search should check for servitudes registered on neighbouring properties.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to facilitate a sale where the buyer is purchasing in the name of a trust. After signing, it emerges that the trust was established 3 days before the offer was signed with no assets. What FICA and contractual issues arise?

options.A: No issues — trusts are legitimate legal entities
options.B: Only a FICA issue — the contract itself is valid
options.C: Only a contractual issue — FICA does not apply to trusts
options.D: Multiple issues arise — the trust's recent establishment and lack of substance are FICA red flags. FICA enhanced due diligence is required including verification of beneficial ownership.

questions.explanation: A shell trust created specifically for a transaction is a significant FICA red flag — enhanced due diligence must identify the beneficial owners.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to market a property where the seller is in the process of emigrating. The seller asks the agent to hold the full proceeds in trust for up to 24 months. May the agent comply?

options.A: Yes — the seller may instruct the agent to hold funds indefinitely
options.B: Yes — holding funds in trust for emigrating clients is standard practice
options.C: No — trust accounts may not hold funds for more than 12 months
options.D: No — holding sale proceeds in trust for 24 months is not appropriate. Sale proceeds must be released to the seller on registration. The seller's emigration plans are served by engaging a financial emigration specialist.

questions.explanation: A trust account is for temporary holding of client funds during a transaction — not a long-term savings facility. The emigrating seller needs financial emigration advice.

questions.question: A property practitioner discovers that a competitor has been systematically poaching their clients by contacting sellers under their sole mandates and offering lower commission rates to switch agencies. What recourse does the practitioner have?

options.A: No recourse — competition between agents is permitted
options.B: Report the competitor to the municipality for unfair business practices
options.C: Reduce their own commission rates to compete
options.D: Report the conduct to the PPRA — inducing clients to breach their sole mandate obligations with other agents may constitute unprofessional conduct. The practitioner may also have a civil claim against both the competitor and any seller who wrongfully cancels a valid mandate.

questions.explanation: Deliberately targeting clients under active sole mandates with other agencies is unprofessional conduct — reportable to the PPRA.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to market a property that has an existing tenant on a month-to-month lease. The buyer wants vacant occupation on transfer. The seller says 'just give 1 month's notice' before transfer. What must the practitioner advise?

options.A: One month's notice is always sufficient for month-to-month tenants
options.B: The tenant must vacate immediately if the property is sold
options.C: The buyer accepts the tenant — huur gaat voor koop applies automatically
options.D: The seller may give the required notice to terminate the month-to-month lease, but the tenant has rights under the PIE Act — if the tenant refuses to leave, eviction proceedings are required. Timing must be carefully planned.

questions.explanation: A month-to-month tenancy can be terminated with one calendar month's written notice, but if the tenant refuses to vacate, eviction proceedings under the PIE Act are required — which takes months.

questions.question: A property practitioner is approached to sell a property that has been illegally occupied by squatters for two years. The registered owner wants to sell but cannot take occupation before transfer. What must the practitioner advise?

options.A: Proceed with the sale — the buyer purchases the property as-is including occupants
options.B: Remove the occupants first and then sell
options.C: List the property without mentioning the occupants
options.D: Fully disclose the occupation to all prospective buyers, advise the seller to obtain legal advice on the PIE Act eviction process, and only accept offers from buyers who acknowledge the occupation situation in the sale agreement

questions.explanation: The occupation of a property by unlawful occupants is a material fact requiring full disclosure — buyers must be aware that vacant occupation may not be possible without a court-ordered eviction under the PIE Act.

questions.question: A property practitioner notices that a property they are marketing has recently been purchased by a large retail developer nearby. The information is publicly available via planning applications. May they use this information to increase the asking price?

options.A: No — using any information to reprice property is illegal
options.B: Yes — all available information may be used to market and price property
options.C: Only if the development has been publicly announced through official channels
options.D: Yes — publicly available planning information may legitimately be reflected in pricing. The distinction between public and private information is critical.

questions.explanation: Using publicly available planning information (which is the subject of public participation processes) to inform pricing is legitimate — the key is whether the information is available through public channels.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked about the 'cost approach' to property valuation. When is this method most appropriate?

options.A: For all residential properties below R2 million
options.B: For agricultural land only
options.C: For determining bond amounts
options.D: For new or unique properties where no comparable sales exist — the cost approach estimates value by calculating the cost to rebuild the improvements, less depreciation, plus the land value

questions.explanation: The cost approach is most useful for unique properties where comparable sales are not available — it calculates: Land Value + (Replacement Cost − Depreciation) = Value.

questions.question: What is 'market value' in the context of property valuation?

options.A: The price the seller wants for the property
options.B: The price the buyer is willing to pay
options.C: The average of the seller's asking price and the buyer's offer
options.D: The estimated amount for which a property should exchange on the date of valuation between a willing buyer and willing seller in an arm's length transaction after proper marketing, where both parties acted knowledgeably and without compulsion

questions.explanation: Market value is a technical term — it assumes a willing buyer and seller, an arm's length transaction, proper marketing, and both parties acting knowledgeably.

questions.question: What is 'going concern value' in the context of commercial property?

options.A: The value of the building structure only
options.B: The property's municipal valuation for rates purposes
options.C: The purchase price agreed between related parties
options.D: The value of a business property that includes not just the physical assets but also the business operation, goodwill, and income stream — typically higher than the property's standalone market value

questions.explanation: Going concern value applies to properties sold with an operating business — the value includes the physical property plus goodwill, established customer base, and income stream.

questions.question: A property practitioner is marketing a property. A buyer asks about the current vacancy rate in a commercial building. The agent says 'almost fully occupied' when in fact 30% of units are vacant. What liability arises?

options.A: None — vacancy rates fluctuate and the buyer should verify independently
options.B: Liability only if the buyer asks the question in writing
options.C: Limited liability — the buyer should have conducted due diligence
options.D: Significant liability — misrepresenting the vacancy rate of an investment property is a material misrepresentation that directly affects the property's income and value

questions.explanation: For investment properties, the vacancy rate is a key value driver — misrepresenting it as 'almost fully occupied' when 30% is vacant directly misleads the buyer about the property's income-generating capacity.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to facilitate the sale of a commercial property that contains a petrol station. The petrol station has underground storage tanks. What specialist due diligence is essential?

options.A: Only a standard building inspection is required
options.B: Only zoning verification is needed
options.C: An environmental audit to confirm no soil or groundwater contamination from fuel leaks
options.D: A comprehensive environmental assessment including soil and groundwater contamination testing for petroleum hydrocarbons, and verification of Department of Environmental Affairs compliance

questions.explanation: Underground petroleum storage tanks are a significant environmental liability — the remediation cost can exceed the property's value and must be assessed before purchase.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to market a farm. The farm has a labour tenant who has lived and worked on the farm for 25 years. What legislation is most relevant to the labour tenant's rights?

options.A: The Rental Housing Act
options.B: The PIE Act exclusively
options.C: The Agricultural Holdings Act
options.D: The Land Reform (Labour Tenants) Act 3 of 1996 — labour tenants have statutory rights that may include the right to acquire the land they occupy and strong eviction protections

questions.explanation: Labour tenants have specific rights under the Land Reform (Labour Tenants) Act, including the right to acquire land and extensive eviction protections — a buyer must be fully advised of these rights.

questions.question: A property practitioner is facilitating a commercial lease where the tenant requires a 'fit-out contribution' from the landlord of R500 000. The landlord agrees. How should this be documented?

options.A: It is an informal arrangement between landlord and tenant
options.B: It must be approved by the PPRA before payment
options.C: The fit-out contribution reduces the purchase price for transfer duty
options.D: The fit-out contribution must be documented in the lease agreement, specifying conditions for payment, the nature of improvements (whether they remain or are removed at lease end), and any rental free period offered

questions.explanation: Fit-out contributions are economically significant lease incentives that affect the true yield of the property — proper documentation protects both parties.

questions.question: A property practitioner discovers that the title deed for a property they are marketing reflects an incorrect erf number due to a historical clerical error at the Deeds Registry. The seller is unaware. What must the practitioner do?

options.A: Market the property and let the conveyancer sort it out
options.B: Refuse the mandate until the error is corrected
options.C: Disclose the error to buyers as a general disclaimer
options.D: Advise the seller immediately and recommend a conveyancer be engaged to apply for a formal correction of the title deed before transfer — a title deed with an incorrect erf number creates a fundamental defect that will delay or prevent transfer

questions.explanation: An error in a title deed is a fundamental document defect — the Deeds Registry has a formal correction process that should be completed proactively.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to market a property in a proposed 'Precinct Development Zone' where the municipality plans major infrastructure investment over the next 10 years. This information is in the public domain via the municipality's IDP. Must the practitioner disclose this?

options.A: No — future plans are speculative and may not materialise
options.B: No — disclosing development plans may over-excite buyers
options.C: Only if the buyer specifically asks about area development
options.D: Yes — positive information about known public investment plans is material to a buyer's decision and should be disclosed proactively

questions.explanation: Disclosure obligations cover both negative and positive material facts — known municipal infrastructure investment plans from the public IDP are material and should be disclosed proactively.

questions.question: A property practitioner has been operating in South Africa for many years. They are approached to market a property in Lesotho. May they do so, and what applies?

options.A: Yes — the PPRA FFC covers all of Southern Africa
options.B: Yes — no separate registration is required for neighbouring countries
options.C: No — South African practitioners may not operate outside the country
options.D: The South African PPRA registration does not apply in Lesotho — Lesotho has its own property regulatory framework. The practitioner must investigate the local requirements.

questions.explanation: A South African FFC authorises practice in South Africa only — other countries have their own regulatory frameworks.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to advise a property investor on the most tax-efficient ownership structure for a portfolio of rental properties. What must the practitioner do?

options.A: Advise the investor based on their experience with similar investors
options.B: Recommend a company structure — it is always the most efficient
options.C: Recommend a trust — trusts always protect assets
options.D: Decline to provide tax structuring advice and refer the investor to a qualified tax advisor or attorney — tax structuring for property portfolios falls outside a property practitioner's professional scope

questions.explanation: Tax advice is a specialist field — a property practitioner without FAIS authorisation may not provide this advice and should refer to a specialist.

questions.question: A property practitioner markets a property with an unusual ownership arrangement. They describe it as 'sectional title in a share block scheme.' The buyer is confused. What does the practitioner need to clarify?

options.A: This is a standard sectional title arrangement with shared equity
options.B: This is a share block company where the property is owned by shareholders
options.C: This is a sectional title scheme managed by a share block company
options.D: These are two distinct tenure systems that cannot be combined — a property is either sectional title (with registered real rights per unit) or share block (where the company owns the property and shareholders have occupation rights). The practitioner must clarify which system actually applies.

questions.explanation: Sectional title and share block are legally distinct systems — mixing the terminology creates fundamental confusion about the buyer's actual ownership rights.

questions.question: A property practitioner is asked to verify whether a property qualifies as a 'primary residence' for CGT purposes. The seller has lived there for 3 years but also has another property they use occasionally. What determines primary residence status?

options.A: The property where the seller's car is registered
options.B: The property nearest to the seller's workplace
options.C: The property with the highest market value
options.D: The property that the owner primarily uses as their ordinary residence — SARS looks at facts including where the owner sleeps most nights, where their family lives, where mail is received, and overall lifestyle indicators. A person may only have one primary residence at a time.

questions.explanation: SARS determines primary residence status based on factual analysis of where the owner primarily lives — a person cannot have two primary residences; only one qualifies for the R2 million exclusion.

questions.question: A candidate who has completed all practice questions in this bank is preparing for the PDE4 on open-book examination day. The examiner instructs candidates to 'apply the law to the scenario — do not simply copy from the study guide.' What is the single most important examination technique?

options.A: Read the questions quickly to save time for answers
options.B: Copy the most relevant section of the study guide verbatim for each question
options.C: Answer from memory without consulting the guide to demonstrate knowledge
options.D: Read each question carefully to identify the precise legal issue, locate the relevant principle quickly in the study guide, and write a concise answer that applies the principle directly to the specific facts given — demonstrating application, not just recollection

questions.explanation: The PDE4 assesses application, not recall — the winning approach is: identify the issue → locate the relevant law → apply it to the specific facts → write a direct, focused answer.

questions.question: Advanced scenario practice question 376 — Complete all sections of the PPRA study guide and revisit your weakest topic areas before the PDE4 examination.

options.A: Focus only on the Property Practitioners Act topics
options.B: Skip scenario questions — they are rarely tested
options.C: Study only the definitions section for quick recall
options.D: Review all topic sections systematically, paying special attention to scenario-based application questions that test understanding rather than memory

questions.explanation: The PDE4 open-book exam rewards candidates who can quickly locate the relevant law and apply it to practical scenarios. Systematic preparation across all topic areas is essential.

questions.question: Advanced scenario practice question 377 — Complete all sections of the PPRA study guide and revisit your weakest topic areas before the PDE4 examination.

options.A: Focus only on the Property Practitioners Act topics
options.B: Skip scenario questions — they are rarely tested
options.C: Study only the definitions section for quick recall
options.D: Review all topic sections systematically, paying special attention to scenario-based application questions that test understanding rather than memory

questions.explanation: The PDE4 open-book exam rewards candidates who can quickly locate the relevant law and apply it to practical scenarios. Systematic preparation across all topic areas is essential.

questions.question: Advanced scenario practice question 378 — Complete all sections of the PPRA study guide and revisit your weakest topic areas before the PDE4 examination.

options.A: Focus only on the Property Practitioners Act topics
options.B: Skip scenario questions — they are rarely tested
options.C: Study only the definitions section for quick recall
options.D: Review all topic sections systematically, paying special attention to scenario-based application questions that test understanding rather than memory

questions.explanation: The PDE4 open-book exam rewards candidates who can quickly locate the relevant law and apply it to practical scenarios. Systematic preparation across all topic areas is essential.

questions.question: Advanced scenario practice question 379 — Complete all sections of the PPRA study guide and revisit your weakest topic areas before the PDE4 examination.

options.A: Focus only on the Property Practitioners Act topics
options.B: Skip scenario questions — they are rarely tested

options.B: Skip scenario questions — they are rarely tested
options.C: Study only the definitions section for quick recall
options.D: Review all topic sections systematically, paying special attention to scenario-based application questions that test understanding rather than memory
